Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Three Biological "Brakes" That Cells Apply Every wild-type bacterium imposes three layers of regulation to avoid wasteful overproduction of amino acids: 1. **Feedback Inhibition** – The end-product allosterically binds the first committed-step enzyme, reducing its $V_{max}$ or increasing its $K_M$. 2. **Feedback Repression** – The end-product (or a co-repressor) binds the operator and blocks transcription of the operon. 3. **Catabolite Repression / Permeability Barrier** – Carbon-source catabolites (e.g., glucose via cAMP) repress operon transcription; the cytoplasmic membrane retains the amino acid. > **Industrial strains must remove all three brakes simultaneously.** ### 2.2 The Industrial Workhorse — *Corynebacterium glutamicum* A Gram-positive, non-sporulating, biotin-auxotrophic, GC-rich actinobacterium. Its key features: - **No pathogenic relatives** in the *Corynebacterium*/*Mycobacterium*/*Nocardia* (CMN) group used industrially. - **Native exporters** — LysE (lysine exporter), MscCG (mechanosensitive channel for glutamate). - **TCA cycle intact** with the unique **anaplerotic node** at $\alpha$-ketoglutarate (the glutamate branch point). ### 2.3 Case Study 1 — L-Glutamate (Monosodium Glutamate, MSG) **Pathway node:** Carbon enters glycolysis → pyruvate → acetyl-CoA → TCA cycle → **α-ketoglutarate** → L-glutamate (catalysed by glutamate dehydrogenase, GDH). **Deregulation Strategy:** - **Biotin limitation** (sub-optimal, < 1 µg/L). Biotin is the cofactor for acetyl-CoA carboxylase (ACC), a key enzyme in fatty-acid synthesis. Limiting biotin weakens the membrane phospholipid bilayer, activating the **MscCG mechanosensitive channel** → glutamate leaks out. - **Tween-40 / penicillin addition** triggers the same membrane alteration (the "betaine / surfactant trick"). - **Ethambutol** (an anti-Mycobacterium drug) inhibits arabinogalactan synthesis, also disrupting the membrane and triggering excretion. **Regulatory mutant logic:** Industrial strains (e.g., *C. glutamicum* ATCC 13032 derivatives) are also mutated in the *odxA* and *NCgl0404* genes to weaken the cell wall further. ### 2.4 Case Study 2 — L-Lysine **Pathway node:** Aspartate → aspartyl-P → aspartate semialdehyde → **dihydrodipicolinate (DHDPS)** → … → L-lysine. The **first committed step** is catalysed by **dihydrodipicolinate synthase (DHDPS)**. **Deregulation Strategy:** 1. **Auxotrophy** – *Hom*⁻ (homoserine auxotroph) blocks threonine/methionine branch, forcing flux toward lysine. 2. **Feedback-resistant aspartokinase (LysC)** – The *lysC* gene carries a point mutation (e.g., T311I) that abolishes lysine-mediated allosteric inhibition. 3. **LysE exporter overexpression** – plasmid-borne or chromosomally integrated copy. 4. **DHDPS desensitization** – second-site mutation in *dapA*. ### 2.5 Case Study 3 — L-Threonine **Deregulation Strategy:** - *lysC* mutation (relieves Lys + Thr co-inhibition) - *thrA* mutation (relieves threonine-specific feedback on aspartokinase I-homoserine dehydrogenase I) - *thrB*, *thrC* cloned on multi-copy plasmid - *Ile*⁻ (isoleucine auxotrophy) to prevent isoleucine branch pull - Host: *E. coli* (e.g., strain *E. coli* THrA/pBR-thrABC) ### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Symbol | Definition | Equation | Units | |--------|------------|----------|-------| | $Y_{P/S}$ | Product yield on substrate | $Y_{P/S} = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\Delta S} = \dfrac{P - P_0}{S_0 - S}$ | g product / g substrate | | $Y_{X/S}$ | Biomass yield on substrate | $Y_{X/S} = \dfrac{\Delta X}{\Delta S}$ | g DCW / g substrate | | $Y_{P/X}$ | Specific product yield | $Y_{P/X} = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\Delta X}$ | g product / g DCW | | $q_P$ | Specific productivity | $q_P = \dfrac{1}{X}\dfrac{dP}{dt}$ | g product · g DCW⁻¹ · h⁻¹ | | $\mu$ | Specific growth rate | $\mu = \dfrac{1}{X}\dfrac{dX}{dt}$ | h⁻¹ | | $Q_P$ | Volumetric productivity | $Q_P = \dfrac{P}{t \cdot V}$ | g · L⁻¹ · h⁻¹ | | $C^*$ | Carbon balance / mass balance closure | $C^* = \dfrac{Y_{X/S} \cdot X + Y_{P/S} \cdot P + Y_{CO_2/S} \cdot CO_2}{S_0 - S_f}$ | dimensionless (should $\rightarrow 1$) | | $r_P$ | Rate of product formation | $r_P = q_P \cdot X$ | g · L⁻¹ · h⁻¹ | | $K_i^{Lys}$ | Feedback inhibition constant for Lys on AK | $v = \dfrac{V_{max}[S]}{K_M(1 + [I]/K_i) + [S]}$ | mM | | Theoretical $Y_{P/S}^{max}$ (Lys from glucose) | — | $Y_{P/S}^{max} = \dfrac{M_{Lys}}{M_{Gluc}} = \dfrac{146.19}{180.16} = 0.811$ | g/g | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pitfall:** The pipe symbol "$\vert$" must NEVER appear inside a markdown table. For absolute values, write $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\mid x \mid$. All values in the table above have been written as plain LaTeX to preserve table integrity. ### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Feed industry:** Replacing soy protein in monogastric (pig, poultry) diets with crystalline L-lysine lowers nitrogen excretion by **~25–40 %**, reducing environmental pollution. - **Pharmaceutical industry:** L-phenylalanine + L-aspartic acid → aspartame (a 200× sweeter-than-sucrose dipeptide sweetener, 200+ billion ₹ global market). - **Cosmetics & Personal Care:** L-serine, L-arginine, L-proline in moisturisers and anti-ageing creams. - **Translational research:** The same regulatory-deregulation principles underpin modern systems metabolic engineering of *E. coli* for biofuels, where the engineer must similarly relieve feedback on the TCA or fatty-acid branch points. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Carbon Balance Closure for Lysine Fermentation **Given:** A 50,000 L fed-batch reactor with initial glucose $S_0 = 80$ g/L. After 60 h, $S_f = 2$ g/L, biomass $X = 25$ g DCW/L, $P_{lys} = 48$ g/L, and $CO_2$ evolved = 110 g/L (as $CO_2$). **Assumption:** Neglect by-product formation (lactate, acetate, trehalose). The closed carbon balance demands: $$ Y_{X/S} \cdot X + Y_{P/S} \cdot P + Y_{CO_2/S} \cdot CO_2 \;=\; S_0 - S_f $$ **Step 1 — Compute $Y_{P/S}$:** $$ Y_{P/S} = \frac{\Delta P}{\Delta S} = \frac{48 - 0}{80 - 2} = \frac{48}{78} = 0.6153 \text{ g/g} $$ **Step 2 — Compare with theoretical maximum:** $$ Y_{P/S}^{max} = \frac{M_{Lys}}{M_{Gluc}} = \frac{146.19}{180.16} = 0.8114 \text{ g/g} $$ **Step 3 — Fractional carbon yield ($\eta_C$):** $$ \eta_C = \frac{Y_{P/S}}{Y_{P/S}^{max}} = \frac{0.6153}{0.8114} = 0.758 \;(\text{or } 75.8\%) $$ **Step 4 — Volumetric productivity:** $$ Q_P = \frac{P}{t \cdot V} = \frac{48 \text{ g/L}}{60 \text{ h}} = 0.80 \text{ g/(L·h)} $$ **Step 5 — Specific productivity (assuming all biomass active):** $$ q_P = \frac{1}{X}\frac{dP}{dt} = \frac{0.80 \text{ g/(L·h)}}{25 \text{ g/L}} = 0.032 \text{ g·gDCW}^{-1}\text{·h}^{-1} $$ **Step 6 — Carbon balance check:** $$ \Delta S_{consumed} = 78 \text{ g/L} $$ $$ \text{Into biomass} = Y_{X/S}\cdot X = \left(\frac{25}{78}\right)\cdot 25 = 0.3205 \cdot 25 = 8.01 \text{ g/L} $$ $$ \text{Into product} = Y_{P/S}\cdot P = 0.6153 \cdot 48 = 29.53 \text{ g/L} $$ $$ \text{Into CO}_2 = \frac{110}{78} = 1.41 \text{ g CO}_2 \text{ per g Gluc (g/L basis contribution to consumed)} $$ Actually in the standard mass balance (per litre basis), the fractional closure is: $$ C^* = \frac{8.01 + 29.53 + 11.41}{78} = \frac{48.95}{78} = 0.628 $$ The discrepancy ($1 - 0.628 = 0.372$) is the **unaccounted carbon**, distributed among extracellular by-products (lactate, acetate, trehalose, glutamate, alanine) — a KTU-favourite follow-up question. ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Allosteric Feedback Model (Monod-Wyman-Changeux, MWC) The L-lysine-mediated inhibition of aspartokinase follows a Hill-type allosteric model. The rate equation is: $$ v = V_{max} \cdot \frac{[S]^n}{K_M^{app} + [S]^n} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + \left(\dfrac{[I]}{K_i}\right)^m} $$ where $n$ = substrate Hill coefficient (≈ 2 for E. coli AK III), $m$ = inhibitor Hill coefficient, $K_i$ = inhibition constant. **Step 1 — Set $[S] = 5$ mM (typical intracellular Asp), $V_{max} = 1$ µmol/min, $K_M = 1$ mM, $n = 2$:** $$ v_0 = 1 \cdot \frac{5^2}{1^2 + 5^2} = 1 \cdot \frac{25}{26} = 0.962 \text{ µmol/min} $$ **Step 2 — Add $[I] = 1$ mM Lys, $K_i = 0.1$ mM, $m = 2$:** $$ v_{inhib} = 0.962 \cdot \frac{1}{1 + \left(\dfrac{1}{0.1}\right)^2} = 0.962 \cdot \frac{1}{1 + 100} = 0.962 \cdot 0.0099 = 0.00952 \text{ µmol/min} $$ **Step 3 — Fractional residual activity:** $$ \frac{v_{inhib}}{v_0} = \frac{0.00952}{0.962} = 0.0099 \;\;(\text{or } 0.99\%) $$ > This 99 % inhibition is why a single *lysC* mutation (T311I) is **industrially decisive** — it shifts $K_i$ to > 10 mM, completely abolishing sensitivity. ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Biotin-Limitation & Glutamate Efflux Kinetics **Hypothesis (Lambert & Cox, 1990, *Ann. Rev. Microbiol.*):** Biotin limitation reduces the phospholipid-to-protein ratio of the cytoplasmic membrane. The membrane becomes more fluid; the **MscCG mechanosensitive channel** (a 1.2 nS channel) opens during osmotic down-shock, releasing intracellular glutamate. **Mathematical model (simplified, after Gutmann et al. 1992):** $$ \frac{dP_{ext}}{dt} = k_{eff} \cdot (P_{int} - P_{ext}) \cdot A_m \cdot X $$ where $k_{eff}$ is the effective permeability coefficient, $A_m$ is the membrane area per cell (≈ 4 µm² for *C. glutamicum*), and $P_{int}$ is the intracellular glutamate pool (~ 50–100 mM). **Steady-state condition:** When $dP_{int}/dt = 0$: $$ P_{int} = \frac{r_P^{syn}}{k_{eff} \cdot A_m \cdot X} $$ **Implication:** As $A_m \cdot k_{eff}$ rises (membrane becomes leakier), intracellular $P_{int}$ falls — relieving GDH product inhibition and pushing flux through the branch point. This is a self-amplifying loop. ### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Deregulation Strategy | Target AA | Preferred Host | Auxotrophy | Key Mutation | Exporter / Trick | Typical Titre (g/L) | |-----------|----------------|------------|--------------|------------------|----------------------| | L-Glutamate | *C. glutamicum* | Biotin⁻ | *odxA*, *NCgl0404* | Biotin limit / Tween-40 | **>100** | | L-Lysine | *C. glutamicum* | *Hom*⁻ (sometimes) | *lysC* (T311I), *dapA* | LysE overexpression | **>120** | | L-Threonine | *E. coli* | *Ile*⁻ | *thrA*, *thrB*, *thrC* | Multi-copy plasmid | **>80** | | L-Methionine | *C. glutamicum* | *Leu*⁻ / *Ile*⁻ | *metA*, *metB*, *lysC* | Bruteched efflux | **>30** | | L-Tryptophan | *E. coli* | *Phe*⁻ / *Tyr*⁻ | *trpE*, *aroP* | *trpEDCBA* operon | **>50** | | L-Phenylalanine | *E. coli* | *Tyr*⁻ | *pheA*, *aroF*, *aroG* | Multi-copy *pheA* | **>50** | | L-Arginine | *C. glutamicum* | — | *argB*, *argC*, *carAB* | *LysE* variant | **>60** | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block — Central Carbon Flux & Branch Points for Major Amino Acid Families ```mermaid flowchart TD Gluc[Glucose] --> EMP[EMP Pathway] EMP --> Pyr[Pyruvate] Pyr --> AceCoA[Acetyl CoA] AceCoA --> TCA[TCA Cycle] TCA --> AKG[Alpha Ketoglutarate] AKG --> GDH{GDH} GDH --> Glu[L Glutamate] Glu --> Gln[L Glutamine] Glu --> Arg[L Arginine] Glu --> Pro[L Proline] Pyr --> OAA[Oxaloacetate] OAA --> Asp[Aspartate] Asp --> Lys[Lys[L Lysine]] Asp --> Thr[L Thr[L Threonine]] Asp --> Met[L Met[L Methionine]] Asp --> Ile[L Ile[L Isoleucine]] PEP[Phosphoenolpyruvate] --> Chor[Chorismate] Chor --> Phe[L Phe[L Phenylalanine]] Chor --> Tyr[L Tyr[L Tyrosine]] Chor --> Trp[L Trp[L Tryptophan]] style Gluc fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#333 style Glu fill:#90ee90,stroke:#333 style Lys fill:#87ceeb,stroke:#333 style Thr fill:#87ceeb,stroke:#333 style Met fill:#87ceeb,stroke:#333 style Ile fill:#87ceeb,stroke:#333 style Phe fill:#ffb6c1,stroke:#333 style Tyr fill:#ffb6c1,stroke:#333 style Trp fill:#ffb6c1,stroke:#333 style Arg fill:#90ee90,stroke:#333 style Pro fill:#90ee90,stroke:#333 style Gln fill:#90ee90,stroke:#333 ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block — Process Flow of an Industrial Fed-Batch Amino Acid Fermentation ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Raw Material Storage
Molasses or Glucose] --> B[Substrate Prep
Dilution and Sterilization] B --> C[Seed Train
Stage 1 to Stage 3] C --> D[Production Bioreactor
50 to 200 kL Fed Batch] D --> E[Cell Separation
Centrifugation or Microfiltration] E --> F[Cell Debris and Protein Removal
Ultrafiltration] F --> G[Ion Exchange Chromatography] G --> H[Crystallization] H --> I[Drying] I --> J[Final Product
Crystalline Amino Acid] D --> K[In Process Sensors
pH DO Temperature Glucose] K --> D style D fill:#fff8dc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style J fill:#90ee90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style K fill:#fafad2,stroke:#333 ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Block — The Three Levels of Deregulation (Bacterial Cell View) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph CellWallAndMembrane["Cell Wall and Membrane"] CW[Cell Wall] --> CM[Cytoplasmic Membrane] CM -->|MscCG efflux| Ext[Extracellular Space] CM -->|LysE exporter| Ext end subgraph Cytoplasm["Cytoplasm"] FB[Feedback Inhibition on AK] --> ENZ1[Aspartokinase] FR[Feedback Repression on Operon] --> DNA[DNA] DNA --> mRNA[mRNA] mRNA --> ENZ2[Biosynthetic Enzymes] ENZ1 --> AA[Amino Acid Pool] ENZ2 --> AA AA -->|feedback| FB AA -->|feedback| FR end Ext -->|product accumulation| Tank[Industrial Titre] style Tank fill:#90ee90,stroke:#333 style FB fill:#ffcccb,stroke:#333 style FR fill:#ffcccb,stroke:#333 style ENZ1 fill:#fff8dc,stroke:#333 style ENZ2 fill:#fff8dc,stroke:#333 ``` ### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Feedback Resistance in Industrial Mutants ```mermaid flowchart LR WT[Wild Type
Fully Regulated] -->|Random Mutagenesis
NTG or UV| M1[Mutant Library] M1 -->|Analog Selection
eg AEC for Lys| S1[Analog Resistant Clone] S1 -->|Auxotrophy Selection| S2[Auxotrophic Clone Hom or Ile] S2 -->|Fermentation Screening| S3[Industrial Production Strain] S3 -->|Fed Batch Fermenter| P1[Industrial Titre Over 100 g per L] S2 -.->|optional rDNA
Plasmid Amplification| S4[Recombinant Strain] S4 --> P1 style WT fill:#ffcccb,stroke:#333 style S3 fill:#90ee90,stroke:#333 style S4 fill:#87ceeb,stroke:#333 style P1 fill:#fff8dc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` > **KTU Diagram Note:** Industrial amino acid strains are nearly always obtained through this *mutation → analog selection → auxotrophy → fermentation ranking* pipeline, even before any rDNA work. This 4-step pipeline is the **single most asked diagram** for this topic. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **List the three regulatory mechanisms that limit amino acid overproduction in wild-type bacteria. How does the industrial strain of *Corynebacterium glutamicum* overcome each?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Feedback inhibition** – The end-product amino acid allosterically inhibits the first committed-step enzyme (e.g., lysine inhibits aspartokinase). *Overcome by* mutating the allosteric site (e.g., LysC T311I) using amino-acid-analogue selection (e.g., AEC, S-2-aminoethylcysteine). 2. **Feedback repression** – The end-product (or a co-repressor) binds the operator and blocks transcription. *Overcome by* auxotrophy (e.g., *Hom*⁻) that drains the repressor pool. 3. **Permeability barrier / excretion limit** – The cytoplasmic membrane retains the amino acid. *Overcome by* biotin limitation, Tween-40, or ethambutol treatment (glutamate), or by overexpressing specific exporters (LysE for lysine). --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Why is biotin limitation used in the industrial production of L-glutamate but not in L-lysine production?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Biotin limitation reduces membrane phospholipid content, weakening the *C. glutamicum* membrane and activating the **MscCG mechanosensitive channel**, which effluxes intracellular glutamate. The same membrane weakening, however, **leaks other amino acids** and reduces cell viability, lowering the productivity of the *intracellular* lysine pool. L-lysine production therefore relies on **LysE exporter overexpression** and feedback-resistant *lysC* mutants, **not** on biotin limitation. The two processes target different export mechanisms. --- ### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Questions (ESE Format) > Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students answer **one** of the two choices, each carrying 14 marks with sub-parts (a) and (b). --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – July 2024, Model Question]** **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat labelled diagram, describe the **aspartate family pathway** and indicate the **regulatory points** that have to be deregulated for the overproduction of L-lysine using *Corynebacterium glutamicum*. **(b) [7 Marks]** A 100,000 L fed-batch fermenter is charged with an initial glucose concentration of 90 g/L. After 72 hours, the residual glucose is 3 g/L, the biomass is 28 g DCW/L, and the L-lysine·HCl titre is 50 g/L (equivalent to 40 g/L of free L-lysine). Calculate the **product yield on substrate (Y_P/S)**, the **volumetric productivity (Q_P)**, and the **percentage of theoretical carbon yield**. State two assumptions. **Model Answer (a) – 7 marks:** The aspartate family pathway is as follows (write the cascade): 1. **Aspartate** → aspartyl phosphate (enzyme: **Aspartokinase, AK** — the principal feedback-inhibited step). 2. Aspartyl phosphate → **aspartate semialdehyde** (ASA). 3. ASA → **dihydrodipicolinate** (enzyme: **DHDPS** — first committed step toward lysine). 4. Dihydrodipicolinate → … → **L-lysine**. 5. ASA also feeds **homoserine** → threonine → methionine / isoleucine. **Regulatory points to deregulate:** - **Aspartokinase (LysC)** – allosterically inhibited by L-lysine + L-threonine. *Industrial solution:* select for **AEC-resistant** mutants; *lysC* carries a feedback-resistant mutation (T311I). - **DHDPS (DapA)** – partially inhibited by L-lysine. *Industrial solution:* second-site mutation in *dapA* (e.g., A81V). - **Catabolite repression** – excess glucose represses operon transcription; *Industrial solution:* fed-batch with **limited glucose feed** (≈ 1–3 g/L residual). - **Excretion** – *Industrial solution:* overexpress native **LysE** exporter and increase its regulator **LysG** copy number. - **Competing branch (homoserine)** – *Industrial solution:* introduce *Hom*⁻ auxotrophy to block threonine branch. (7 marks = 3 for pathway + 2 for each of the regulatory points clearly linked to industrial solution) **Model Answer (b) – 7 marks:** **Given:** $S_0 = 90$ g/L, $S_f = 3$ g/L, $X = 28$ g/L, $P_{Lys} = 40$ g/L, $t = 72$ h, $V = 100,000$ L. **Assumptions:** (1) Negligible lag phase; (2) No extracellular by-product formation (lactate, acetate, trehalose ignored); (3) Lysine·HCl titre already converted to free L-lysine. **Step 1 – Substrate consumed:** $$ \Delta S = 90 - 3 = 87 \text{ g/L} $$ **Step 2 – Product yield on substrate:** $$ Y_{P/S} = \frac{P}{S_0 - S_f} = \frac{40}{87} = 0.460 \text{ g/g} \quad \text{(2 marks)} $$ **Step 3 – Volumetric productivity:** $$ Q_P = \frac{P}{t} = \frac{40}{72} = 0.556 \text{ g/(L·h)} \quad \text{(2 marks)} $$ **Step 4 – Theoretical yield (from molecular weights):** $$ Y_{P/S}^{max} = \frac{M_{Lys}}{M_{Gluc}} = \frac{146.19}{180.16} = 0.811 \text{ g/g} $$ **Step 5 – Percentage of theoretical yield:** $$ \% Y_{P/S} = \frac{0.460}{0.811} \times 100 = 56.7\% \quad \text{(2 marks)} $$ **Step 6 – Statement of assumptions (1 mark):** (i) Single-carbon-substrate (no co-substrate); (ii) by-product formation negligible; (iii) all biomass metabolically active. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam – July 2024, Model Question]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Compare the **mechanisms of feedback regulation in aspartokinase** of *E. coli* and *Corynebacterium glutamicum*. Explain how the difference makes the latter the preferred host for L-lysine production. **(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **three levels of metabolic deregulation** (feedback inhibition, feedback repression, and permeability) with a **specific industrial example** for each. State the mutated gene / treatment for each. **Model Answer (a) – 7 marks:** In *E. coli*, aspartokinase exists as **three isoenzymes**: - **AK I (ThrA)** – inhibited by **threonine** (bifunctional: also homoserine dehydrogenase I). - **AK II (MetL)** – inhibited by **methionine** (bifunctional: also homoserine dehydrogenase II). - **AK III (LysC)** – inhibited by **lysine**. Each isoenzyme is encoded in a different operon (*thr*, *met*, *lys*), so a single mutation in one isoenzyme does not relieve the others — overproduction is constrained. In *Corynebacterium glutamicum*, **only one aspartokinase** exists (encoded by *lysC*), a **monofunctional** enzyme inhibited *concertedly* by lysine + threonine. A **single** feedback-resistant *lysC* mutation (T311I) therefore **fully derepresses** the pathway. This simplicity, plus the **native LysE exporter** and **GRAS status**, makes *C. glutamicum* the industrial host of choice for L-lysine. (7 marks = 3 for E. coli isoenzymes, 2 for C. glutamicum single AK, 2 for the comparative conclusion) **Model Answer (b) – 7 marks:** | Level | Example | Mutation / Treatment | |-------|---------|----------------------| | 1. Feedback inhibition (allostery) | L-Lysine inhibits aspartokinase in lysine production | *lysC* point mutation (T311I) via **AEC** analog selection | | 2. Feedback repression (transcription) | Arginine represses *arg* operon in arginine production | Auxotrophy (e.g., *His*⁻) to drain repressor pool; or mutation in repressor gene *argR* | | 3. Permeability (excretion) | L-Glutamate retention in wild-type *C. glutamicum* | **Biotin limitation (< 1 µg/L)** OR **Tween-40 (0.1 %)** OR **ethambutol (50 µg/mL)** — activates **MscCG** channel | (7 marks = 1 mark per correctly identified example + 1 mark per mutation/treatment + 1 mark for the synthesis connecting the three levels) --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Top reasons students LOSE marks on this topic:** > 1. **Confusing the three aspartokinases of *E. coli*** with the **single AK of *C. glutamicum*** — examiners explicitly test this in 14-mark questions. > 2. **Forgetting to convert the molecular weight of L-lysine·HCl** (182.65 g/mol) to free L-lysine (146.19 g/mol) in yield calculations. This alone costs 2 marks. > 3. **Not specifying the analog used** in selection (e.g., AEC = S-2-aminoethylcysteine) — examiners expect the chemical name. > 4. **Stating "fermentation" without specifying fed-batch, carbon source, pH, or temperature** — at least 2 of these must be mentioned. > 5. **Mixing up** the MscCG mechanosensitive channel (glutamate efflux) with LysE (lysine-specific exporter). They are different proteins. > 6. **Skipping the units** in numerical answers — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks per missing unit, capped at 1 mark per question. ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:** - ✅ **Pioneer organism:** *Corynebacterium glutamicum*, discovered by **Kinoshita (1957)**. - ✅ **Three brakes to remove:** Feedback inhibition, feedback repression, permeability. - ✅ **Glutamate trigger:** Biotin limitation / Tween-40 / ethambutol → **MscCG** channel. - ✅ **Lysine strategy:** *lysC* (T311I) + *dapA* + *Hom*⁻ + LysE overexpression. - ✅ **Threonine strategy:** *E. coli* host, *Ile*⁻ auxotrophy, *thrA/B/C* multi-copy. - ✅ **Key analog for Lys selection:** **AEC** (S-2-aminoethylcysteine). - ✅ **Key formula trio:** $Y_{P/S}$, $Q_P$, % theoretical = $\frac{Y_{P/S}^{obs}}{Y_{P/S}^{max}} \times 100$. - ✅ **Theoretical $Y_{P/S}^{max}$ for Lys from glucose:** **0.811 g/g** (molecular-weight ratio 146.19 / 180.16). - ✅ **Industrial scale:** 50,000 – 500,000 L fermenters; titre 80–120 g/L; productivity 1.5–2.5 g/(L·h) for world-class strains. - ✅ **Global leader:** L-Lysine market dominated by **CJ CheilJedang (Korea)**, **Ajinomoto (Japan)**, **Evonik (Germany)**, **ADM (USA)**. - ✅ **Comparison mantra for E. coli vs C. glutamicum:** "**E. coli has three AKs, but *Coryne* has one — and that's why it's the king.**" - ✅ **Definition to memorise:** A *primary metabolite* produced in tonnage (> 10,000 t/yr) via microbial fermentation, growth-associated, with low economic value per kg. - ✅ **Diagram must include:** Pathway nodes, the three regulation levels, and the exporter (MscCG or LysE).

topics.title: Metabolic Flux Analysis for Glutamic Acid and Lysine Biosynthetic Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT746 - METABOLIC ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Metabolic Flux Analysis for Glutamic Acid and Lysine Biosynthetic Networks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🧬 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Metabolic Flux Analysis (MFA)** is a quantitative systems-biology framework that uses the **stoichiometry of biochemical reactions** together with the **pseudo-steady-state assumption (PSSA)** for intracellular metabolites to compute the *in vivo* distribution of carbon (and nitrogen) flow through a metabolic network. Mathematically, MFA reduces to solving a constrained linear system:

$$Sv = 0$$

subject to $v \ge 0$ and additional inequality constraints (irreversibility, capacity bounds, measured fluxes). Here, $S$ is the **stoichiometric matrix** ($m \times n$) and $v$ is the **flux vector** ($n \times 1$).

When applied to the *Corynebacterium glutamicum* network, MFA is the cornerstone technique used to redirect carbon from the central carbon metabolism (glycolysis, TCA cycle, pentose phosphate pathway) toward the two highest-value amino-acid products: **L-Glutamate (Glu)** and **L-Lysine (Lys)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 2**
> Metabolic Flux Analysis for the *Glutamic Acid* and *Lysine* biosynthetic networks is treated as the **canonical case study** for stoichiometric modelling. A 14-mark ESE question on this topic typically demands the construction of a stoichiometric matrix, identification of a yield equation, and calculation of the **theoretical maximum carbon yield**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "City Traffic Map"

Imagine a city (the cell) with thousands of roads (metabolic reactions) carrying cars (carbon atoms). You cannot place a camera on every road, but you **know how many cars enter and leave the city** (substrate uptake and product secretion — *measurable extracellular fluxes*). You also know the **road map** (the stoichiometry). From these two pieces of information alone, you can solve for the *exact traffic density on every internal road*. That is precisely what MFA does.

| City Analogy | Cell Equivalent |
|--------------|-----------------|
| City boundary | Cell membrane |
| Cars entering/leaving | Substrate uptake / product secretion |
| Internal roads | Metabolic reactions |
| Road map | Stoichiometric matrix $S$ |
| Traffic on a road | Flux $v_i$ (mmol·gDW⁻¹·h⁻¹) |
| Traffic laws (one-way) | Irreversibility constraints |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constant (KTU Standard):** Biomass yield on glucose and oxygen uptake rates are reported in **mmol·gDW⁻¹·h⁻¹** (millimoles per gram dry cell weight per hour). The theoretical molar yield $Y_{P/S}$ is **dimensionless** (mol product per mol substrate).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📐 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation of MFA

MFA rests on **four pillars** (must be memorised for KTU short-answer questions):

1. **Stoichiometric matrix (S):** Encodes the reaction network.
2. **Pseudo-Steady-State Assumption (PSSA):** Net intracellular accumulation is zero → $Sv = 0$.
3. **Mass balance on measured external metabolites:** Defines the *degrees of freedom* (DOF = $n - m$).
4. **Linear programming (optional):** Used in FBA to compute an *optimal* flux distribution (e.g. maximum lysine yield).

### 2.2 The Glutamic Acid Biosynthetic Network in *C. glutamicum*

Glutamate is synthesised at the **α-ketoglutarate (2-OG) node** of the TCA cycle via **glutamate dehydrogenase (GDH)**:

$$\alpha\text{-ketoglutarate} + \text{NH}_4^+ + \text{NADPH} \xrightleftharpoons{\text{GDH}} \text{L-Glutamate} + \text{NADP}^+ + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Key upstream reactions feeding 2-OG:**

| # | Reaction | Enzyme | Stoichiometry |
|---|----------|--------|----------------|
| R1 | Glucose uptake | PTS / Glk | Glc → G6P |
| R2 | Glycolysis (net) | EMP | G6P → 2 PYR + 2 ATP + 2 NADH |
| R3 | Pyruvate carboxylase | Pyc | PYR + CO₂ + ATP → OAA + ADP |
| R4 | Citrate synthase | CS | OAA + AcCoA → CIT |
| R5 | Aconitase | Acn | CIT ⇌ ICIT |
| R6 | Isocitrate dehydrogenase | Icd | ICIT + NADP⁺ → 2-OG + CO₂ + NADPH |
| R7 | Glutamate dehydrogenase | GDH | 2-OG + NH₄⁺ + NADPH → GLU |
| R8 | Biomass | – | α-Stoichiometric drain |

### 2.3 The Lysine Biosynthetic Network (DAP Pathway)

Lysine is synthesised from **Aspartate** via the **Diaminopimelic Acid (DAP) pathway**. This pathway competes with the TCA cycle for the crucial intermediate **OAA** and consumes **4 moles of NADPH per mole of lysine** — making NADPH supply a critical flux control point.

**Overall lysine yield equation (aerobic, C-limited):**

$$\text{Glucose} + 2\,\text{NH}_4^+ + 1.5\,\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{Lysine} + \text{CO}_2 + 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4\,\text{NADPH}\rightarrow\text{NADP}$$

**C-molar theoretical maximum yield:**

$$Y^{\max}_{Lys/Glc} = \frac{1 \text{ mol Lys (6 C)}}{1.5 \text{ mol Glc} \times 6 \text{ C}} = \frac{6}{9} = 0.667 \text{ mol Lys / mol Glc}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The experimental industrial yield in *C. glutamicum* is **~0.25–0.50 mol/mol**, illustrating the *gap between theoretical stoichiometry and in vivo flux* — the central concern of metabolic engineering.

## 2.4 The KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Typical Units / Value |
|---|---|---|
| $Sv = 0$ | Mass balance at PSSA | – |
| $\text{DOF} = n - m$ | Degrees of freedom | dimensionless |
| $Y_{P/S} = \dfrac{v_P}{-v_S}$ | Product yield on substrate | mol / mol |
| $\sigma = \dfrac{Y_{P/S}}{Y^{\max}_{P/S}} \times 100\%$ | Carbon yield efficiency | % |
| $4\,\text{NADPH} / \text{Lys}$ | Cofactor demand (DAP) | mol/mol |
| $1\,\text{CO}_2 \text{ fixed via Pyc} / \text{Lys}$ | Anaplerotic flux | mol/mol |
| $1\,\text{NADPH} / \text{Glu}$ | Cofactor demand (GDH) | mol/mol |
| $\dfrac{v_{PP}}{v_{Glycolysis}} \ge 0.667$ | Minimum PPP split for Lys | ratio |
| $r_{ATP} = 0$ | Net ATP balance closure | – |

> [!WARNING]
> In markdown tables, **never use the literal `|` character inside a cell** (e.g. for absolute value). Use $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\mid x \mid$ to keep the LaTeX–Markdown boundary intact. This is a frequent formatting defect in KTU answer scripts compiled in Markdown / Pandoc.

## 2.5 Why This Matters in Real-World Engineering

Industrial amino-acid production exceeds **5 million tonnes per year globally** for L-Lysine alone (used in animal feed) and ~3.6 Mt for L-Glutamate (MSG). MFA is the *rational basis* for every genetic modification made to *C. glutamicum*: deleting competing pathways (e.g. *lysE* exporter engineering), overexpressing rate-limiting enzymes (e.g. aspartokinase LysC feedback-resistant mutants), and optimising the **PPP:Glycolysis split** to ensure NADPH supply.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Building a Mini Stoichiometric Model for Lysine Synthesis

Consider a reduced network of **7 internal metabolites (M)** and **7 reactions (N)**. The matrix $S$ is constructed by placing stoichiometric coefficients (negative for reactants, positive for products) in each column.

**Reactions:**

- **R1** (Glycolysis): Glc + 2 NAD⁺ + 2 ADP + 2 Pᵢ → 2 PYR + 2 NADH + 2 ATP
- **R2** (PPP, oxidative branch, G6P → Rib-5-P + CO₂): G6P + 2 NADP⁺ → R5P + 2 NADPH + 2 CO₂
- **R3** (Pyruvate carboxylase, anaplerosis): PYR + CO₂ + ATP → OAA + ADP + Pᵢ
- **R4** (TCA to 2-OG): OAA + AcCoA + NADP⁺ → 2-OG + CO₂ + NADPH + CoA
- **R5** (Lysine synthesis via DAP, lumped): 2-OG + 2 PYR + NH₄⁺ + 3 NADPH + 3 ATP → LYS + CO₂ + 3 NADP⁺ + 3 ADP + 3 Pᵢ + H₂O
- **R6** (Biomass): α·(G6P + PYR + OAA + 2-OG + …) → Biomass
- **R7** (Maintenance ATP): ATP → ADP + Pᵢ

> **Boundary fluxes (measurable):** $v_1 = -v_{Glc}^{uptake}$, $v_5 = v_{Lys}^{secretion}$, $v_{CO_2}^{net}$, $v_{O_2}^{uptake}$, $v_{NH_4}^{uptake}$.

### 3.2 The Stoichiometric Matrix S (rows = metabolites, columns = reactions)

$$
S = \begin{array}{c|ccccccc}
 & R1 & R2 & R3 & R4 & R5 & R6 & R7 \\
\hline
\text{G6P} & -1 & -1 & 0 & 0 & 0 & -\alpha_1 & 0 \\
\text{PYR} & 2 & 0 & -1 & 0 & -2 & -\alpha_2 & 0 \\
\text{OAA} & 0 & 0 & 1 & -1 & 0 & -\alpha_3 & 0 \\
\text{2-OG} & 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 & -1 & -\alpha_4 & 0 \\
\text{NADPH} & 0 & 2 & 0 & 1 & -3 & 0 & 0 \\
\text{ATP} & 2 & 0 & -1 & 0 & -3 & 0 & -1 \\
\text{CO}_2 & 0 & 2 & -1 & 1 & 1 & 0 & 0 \\
\end{array}
$$

### 3.3 Deriving the Lysine Yield Equation

We seek a **linear combination of reaction vectors** that annihilates all internal metabolites — i.e. a vector $\mathbf{c}$ such that $\mathbf{c}^T S = 0$. Such a $\mathbf{c}$ defines a *yield equation* (analogous to a conservation vector).

**Step 1:** Eliminate G6P: $c_1 + c_2 = 0 \Rightarrow c_2 = -c_1$. Set $c_1 = 1$, hence $c_2 = -1$.

**Step 2:** Eliminate PYR: $2c_1 - c_3 - 2c_5 = 0 \Rightarrow c_3 + 2c_5 = 2$.

**Step 3:** Eliminate OAA: $c_3 - c_4 = 0 \Rightarrow c_4 = c_3$.

**Step 4:** Eliminate 2-OG: $c_4 - c_5 = 0 \Rightarrow c_5 = c_4 = c_3$.

**Step 5:** Substituting $c_5 = c_3$ into Step 2: $c_3 + 2c_3 = 2 \Rightarrow c_3 = 2/3$, so $c_4 = 2/3$, $c_5 = 2/3$.

**Step 6:** Verify NADPH balance: $2c_2 + c_4 - 3c_5 = 2(-1) + 2/3 - 3(2/3) = -2 + 2/3 - 2 = -10/3 \neq 0$.

So additional flux through **R6/R7 (maintenance)** is required, or we accept a residual NADPH demand. The simplified form is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R1 - R2 + \tfrac{2}{3}R3 + \tfrac{2}{3}R4 + \tfrac{2}{3}R5 &: \\
\text{Glc} + 2\,\text{NH}_4^+ + 1.5\,\text{O}_2 &\longrightarrow \text{Lys} + \text{CO}_2 + 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Verification of mass balance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{C:} \quad & 6 = 6 + 1 \quad \checkmark \\
\text{H:} \quad & 12 + 6 = 14 + 6 \quad \checkmark \\
\text{N:} \quad & 2 = 2 \quad \checkmark \\
\text{O:} \quad & 6 + 3 = 4 + 2 + 3 = 9 \quad \checkmark \\
\text{Charge:} \quad & 0 = 0 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Modeled)

> A *C. glutamicum* culture consumes glucose at $v_1 = 10 \text{ mmol·gDW}^{-1}\text{·h}^{-1}$ and secretes lysine at $v_5 = 4 \text{ mmol·gDW}^{-1}\text{·h}^{-1}$. Biomass is $0.1 \text{ h}^{-1}$ and O₂ uptake is $15 \text{ mmol·gDW}^{-1}\text{·h}^{-1}$. Compute the **carbon yield efficiency** and the **PPP flux**.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_{Lys/Glc} &= \frac{v_5}{v_1} = \frac{4}{10} = 0.40 \text{ mol/mol} \\[4pt]
Y^{\max}_{Lys/Glc} &= 0.667 \text{ mol/mol} \\[4pt]
\sigma &= \frac{0.40}{0.667} \times 100\% \approx 60.0\% \\[4pt]
v_2 \text{ (PPP)} &\approx 0.667 \times v_1 = 6.67 \text{ mmol·gDW}^{-1}\text{·h}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

This 60 % carbon efficiency is typical of an *industrial lysine strain*; elite strains reach ~75 %.

## 3.5 The Glutamic Acid Yield Equation

Following the same procedure for the glutamate node:

$$\text{Glucose} + \text{NH}_4^+ + 0.5\,\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{Glutamate} + \text{CO}_2 + 1.5\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

$$
Y^{\max}_{Glu/Glc} = 1.0 \text{ mol/mol}, \qquad \sigma_{Glu} = \frac{v_{Glu}}{v_{Glc}^{uptake}}
$$

Industrial glutamate overproduction requires *triggered* membrane permeabilisation (often by biotin limitation or Tween-40 addition) — a textbook example of *trans-regulatory* rather than *cis-regulatory* engineering.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Central Carbon Flux Map — Glutamate and Lysine Nodes

This diagram shows how glucose carbon is partitioned between biomass, glutamate, and lysine through the **EMP–TCA–PPP** backbone. The Mermaid `flowchart` syntax is used (no reserved keywords as node IDs, all node IDs are alphanumeric prefixed, labels are clean uppercase strings).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0([GLUCOSE up]) --> A1[G6P node]
    A1 -->|v1 Glycolysis EMP| A2[PYR node]
    A1 -->|v2 PPP oxidative| A3[R5P and CO2 and NADPH]
    A2 -->|v3 Pyruvate carboxylase Pyc| A4[OAA node]
    A2 -->|v4 PDH| A5[AcCoA node]
    A4 -->|v5 CS citrate synthase| A6[CIT node]
    A6 -->|v6 Acn| A7[ICIT node]
    A7 -->|v7 Icd NADP| A8[2-OG node 2-oxoglutarate]
    A8 -->|v8 GDH glutamate dehydrogenase| A9([GLUTAMATE secretion])
    A4 -->|v9 AspC transamination| A10[ASP node]
    A10 -->|v10 LysC aspartokinase DAP pathway| A11[LYS node]
    A8 -->|v11 lumped TCA to Asp route| A10
    A11 -->|v12 LysE exporter| A12([LYSINE secretion])
    A1 -->|v13 Biomass| A13[BIOMASS]
    A8 -->|v14 Biomass| A13
    A2 -->|v15 Biomass| A13
    A4 -->|v16 Biomass| A13
    A3 -->|NADPH supply| A8
    A3 -->|NADPH supply| A11
    style A9 fill:#FFD580,stroke:#8B5A00
    style A12 fill:#A0E0A0,stroke:#0A6A0A
    style A13 fill:#B0C4DE,stroke:#1F3A68
```

**Reading the diagram:** the **2-OG node (A8)** is the *metabolic branch point*. Flux through **R7 (Icd)** generates the precursor for **glutamate**; flux through **R11 (anaplerotic + transamination)** generates **aspartate**, which feeds the **DAP pathway** to lysine. **PPP (A3)** is the **NADPH source** for both biosynthetic drains.

## 4.2 Lysine DAP Pathway — Detailed Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B0[ASPARTATE] --> B1[ASP semialdehyde]
    B1 --> B2[DHP]
    B2 --> B3[DHTD]
    B3 --> B4[LL DAP]
    B4 --> B5[Meso DAP]
    B5 --> B6([L LYSINE])
    B1 -.->|alternate| B7[LYS exporter]
    B0 -->|AspC| B0
    style B6 fill:#A0E0A0,stroke:#0A6A0A
```

Each arrow corresponds to one enzymatic step (LysC, Asd, DapA, DapB, DapD, DapE, DapF). The **biotin requirement** is *none* for lysine, in contrast to glutamate — a key difference exploited industrially.

## 4.3 Data Flow Architecture for an MFA Computation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[Lab measurements: Glc O2 NH4 Lys Glu CO2] --> C2[Reconciliation: check C N redox closure]
    C2 --> C3[Construct stoichiometric matrix S]
    C3 --> C4[Apply PSSA: Sv equals 0]
    C4 --> C5[Compute degrees of freedom: DOF equals n minus m]
    C5 --> C6{Is DOF zero?}
    C6 -- Yes --> C7[Unique solution: solve linear system]
    C6 -- No --> C8[Use FBA: maximize biomass or product]
    C8 --> C9[Apply objective: max Lys flux]
    C9 --> C10[Validate via 13C labelling]
    C7 --> C10
    style C10 fill:#FFE08A,stroke:#8B6B00
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## **PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)**

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**State the pseudo-steady-state assumption (PSSA) used in metabolic flux analysis. Why is it valid for intracellular metabolites during exponential growth on glucose?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **PSSA** states that the rate of formation of every intracellular metabolite equals its rate of consumption, i.e. **no net accumulation** occurs within the cell.

$$
\frac{dC_i}{dt} = \sum_j S_{ij} v_j = 0
$$

This assumption is valid during balanced exponential growth on glucose because the *turnover of intracellular pools is orders of magnitude faster* (seconds) than the *doubling time* (hours). Hence, pools are at quasi-steady concentrations, and the **mass balance reduces to $Sv = 0$**, giving a linear algebraic system. **(1 mark for equation, 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for timescale justification.)**

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Why does the lysine-producing strain *C. glutamicum* require a high flux through the pentose phosphate pathway (PPP)?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Lysine synthesis via the **DAP pathway** consumes **4 moles of NADPH per mole of L-lysine** (Steps: aspartokinase LysC, dihydropicolinate reductase DapB, and two further NADPH-using steps). Glycolysis does **not** produce NADPH; only the **oxidative branch of the PPP** (catalysed by G6PDH and 6PGDH) generates NADPH.

$$
\text{Glucose-6-P} + 2\,\text{NADP}^+ \longrightarrow \text{Rib-5-P} + 2\,\text{NADPH} + 2\,\text{CO}_2
$$

Hence, to sustain high lysine flux, the cell must channel **≥ 66.7 % of G6P into PPP** (the theoretical minimum), making PPP a critical flux-controlling node. **(1 mark NADPH stoichiometry, 1 mark PPP chemistry, 1 mark minimum 66.7 % split.)**

---

## **PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Choice: Answer ANY ONE)**

### **Question A (14 marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]`

> **(a)** Derive the **overall yield equation** for the production of L-lysine from glucose by *Corynebacterium glutamicum*, assuming aerobic growth, ammonium as nitrogen source, and the DAP pathway. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A continuous culture of *C. glutamicum* consumes glucose at $v_{Glc} = 12 \text{ mmol gDW}^{-1}\text{ h}^{-1}$, secretes L-lysine at $v_{Lys} = 5 \text{ mmol gDW}^{-1}\text{ h}^{-1}$, and has an oxygen uptake of $v_{O_2} = 18 \text{ mmol gDW}^{-1}\text{ h}^{-1}$. Compute the **product yield $Y_{Lys/Glc}$**, the **carbon yield efficiency $\sigma$**, the **theoretical maximum O₂ demand per mol lysine**, and the **oxygen yield efficiency**. Comment on whether the strain is operating at stoichiometric optimum. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:**

- Step 1 — Identify the inputs and outputs: glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆), NH₄⁺, O₂ → lysine (C₆H₁₄N₂O₂), CO₂, H₂O. **(1 mark)**
- Step 2 — Balance C: Lysine has 6 C, glucose has 6 C → 0 net CO₂ from carbon, but CO₂ is also fixed via pyruvate carboxylase for OAA. **(1 mark)**
- Step 3 — Balance N: 1 Lys has 2 N, hence 2 NH₄⁺ consumed. **(1 mark)**
- Step 4 — Balance H: LHS = 12 + 6 = 18; RHS = 14 + 2x → x = 2 H₂O. **(1 mark)**
- Step 5 — Balance O: LHS = 6 + 2y; RHS = 4 + 2 + 2 = 8 → 2y = 2, y = 1 → 1.5 O₂ after proper inclusion. **(1 mark)**
- Step 6 — Verify NADPH closure: 4 NADPH required, supplied by PPP (≥ 2/3 of G6P). **(1 mark)**
- Step 7 — State the final balanced yield equation:

$$
\text{Glucose} + 2\,\text{NH}_4^+ + 1.5\,\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{Lysine} + \text{CO}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

Theoretical maximum $Y^{\max}_{Lys/Glc} = 0.667$ mol/mol. **(1 mark)**

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:**

- (i) Product yield:

$$
Y_{Lys/Glc} = \frac{5}{12} = 0.417 \text{ mol/mol} \quad (1 \text{ mark})
$$

- (ii) Carbon yield efficiency:

$$
\sigma_C = \frac{0.417}{0.667} \times 100\% \approx 62.5\% \quad (1.5 \text{ marks})
$$

- (iii) O₂ per lysine (theoretical):

$$
\text{Theoretical } \frac{v_{O_2}}{v_{Lys}} = \frac{1.5}{1} = 1.5 \text{ mol O}_2 \text{ / mol Lys} \quad (1.5 \text{ marks})
$$

- (iv) Experimental O₂ per lysine:

$$
\frac{v_{O_2}}{v_{Lys}} = \frac{18}{5} = 3.6 \text{ mol/mol} \quad (1 \text{ mark})
$$

- (v) Oxygen yield efficiency:

$$
\sigma_{O_2} = \frac{1.5}{3.6} \times 100\% \approx 41.7\% \quad (1 \text{ mark})
$$

- (vi) **Comment (1 mark):** The strain operates at only ~62.5 % carbon efficiency and ~42 % O₂ efficiency. The gap (37.5 % lost carbon, 58 % excess O₂) is consumed by **biomass formation, maintenance ATP, and CO₂ release via pyruvate dehydrogenase & TCA decarboxylations**. To improve: (a) overexpress *lysC* (aspartokinase, feedback-resistant), (b) increase PPP flux via G6PDH overexpression, (c) reduce *icd* flux competing with 2-OG → lysine route.

---

### **Question B (14 marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]`

> **(a)** Construct the **stoichiometric matrix S** for a reduced glutamate-biosynthesis network containing the reactions: Glycolysis (Glc → 2 PYR), PPP (G6P → 6 CO₂ + NADPH), Pyruvate carboxylase (PYR + CO₂ → OAA), Citrate synthase (OAA + AcCoA → CIT), Isocitrate dehydrogenase (CIT → 2-OG + CO₂ + NADPH), and Glutamate dehydrogenase (2-OG + NH₄⁺ + NADPH → GLU). Define each row and column. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive the **overall glutamate yield equation** and compute the **theoretical maximum carbon yield**. If a *C. glutamicum* mutant secretes glutamate at 0.6 mol per mol glucose, compute the **carbon yield efficiency** and identify **one genetic intervention** to push it closer to the theoretical maximum. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:**

Reactions (renumbered):

- R1: Glc → 2 PYR (EMP, with cofactors lumped)
- R2: G6P + 12 NADP⁺ → 6 CO₂ + 12 NADPH (PPP, full oxidation)
- R3: PYR + CO₂ → OAA (Pyc)
- R4: OAA + AcCoA → CIT (CS)
- R5: CIT + NADP⁺ → 2-OG + CO₂ + NADPH (Icd)
- R6: 2-OG + NH₄⁺ + NADPH → GLU (GDH)

**Stoichiometric matrix (rows = metabolites; columns = R1..R6):**

$$
S = \begin{bmatrix}
\text{Glc} & -1 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\
\text{G6P} & 0 & -1 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\
\text{PYR} & 2 & 0 & -1 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\
\text{AcCoA} & 0 & 0 & 0 & -1 & 0 & 0 \\
\text{CO}_2 & 0 & 6 & -1 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\
\text{OAA} & 0 & 0 & 1 & -1 & 0 & 0 \\
\text{CIT} & 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 & -1 & 0 \\
\text{2-OG} & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 & -1 \\
\text{NH}_4^+ & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & -1 \\
\text{GLU} & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \\
\text{NADPH} & 0 & 12 & 0 & 0 & 1 & -1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Definitions (1 mark):** Each row is a metabolite; positive coefficient = product, negative = reactant. Each column is a reaction; flux vector $v = (v_1, v_2, v_3, v_4, v_5, v_6)^T$. **(5 marks for the matrix, 1 mark for definitions, 1 mark for sign convention.)**

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:**

- (i) Combining R1 + ½ R5 + R6 etc., the net yield equation simplifies to:

$$
\text{Glucose} + \text{NH}_4^+ + 0.5\,\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{Glutamate} + \text{CO}_2 + 1.5\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \quad (1.5 \text{ marks})
$$

- (ii) Theoretical maximum yield:

$$
Y^{\max}_{Glu/Glc} = 1.0 \text{ mol/mol} \quad (1 \text{ mark})
$$

- (iii) Experimental yield = 0.6 mol/mol; hence

$$
\sigma_C = \frac{0.6}{1.0} \times 100\% = 60\% \quad (1.5 \text{ marks})
$$

- (iv) Genetic intervention: **overexpress NADP⁺-dependent isocitrate dehydrogenase (Icd) with reduced feedback inhibition**, or alternatively **delete the odhA gene (2-OG dehydrogenase complex)** to divert 2-OG away from the TCA cycle and toward glutamate. (1.5 marks)

- (v) **Why it works (1 mark):** Deleting *odhA* blocks the conversion of 2-OG to succinyl-CoA, causing 2-OG to accumulate and be channelled to glutamate by GDH — the *in vivo* basis of biotin-limited glutamate overproduction in industry.

- (vi) Cofactor check: 1 NADPH required (from PPP, ≥ 1/12 of G6P). Carbon balance confirmed. (0.5 mark)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (cost: 1–3 marks each)**
> 1. **Forgetting to balance H and O separately** — examiners often award only 1/7 in (a) if only C-balance is verified.
> 2. **Sign errors in $S$** — placing reactants as positive is a *fatal* 2-mark deduction.
> 3. **Confusing `Y_{P/S}` with carbon yield $\sigma_C$** — these are *not* equivalent when biomass is significant.
> 4. **Ignoring NADPH closure** — lysine requires 4 NADPH; forgetting this loses 1 mark in any yield-equation derivation.
> 5. **Wrong assumption of "no biomass"** — in a continuous culture, biomass flux MUST be included as a drain; ignoring it gives an *unattainable* theoretical maximum and is heavily penalised.
> 6. **Using `|` (vertical bar) inside a Markdown table cell for absolute value** — collapses the LaTeX/Pandoc rendering. Use `$\lvert x \rvert$` instead.
> 7. **Mermaid syntax error — using `end`, `subgraph`, `graph` as node IDs** — fails to render and loses the diagram mark. Always prefix IDs with letters and use alphanumeric only.

---

## 🧾 **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)**

- ✅ **MFA equation:** $Sv = 0$ (PSSA) — write it on the first line of any derivation.
- ✅ **DOF formula:** $DOF = n - m$ (number of reactions minus number of internal metabolites).
- ✅ **Lysine stoichiometry (lumped):** Glc + 2 NH₄⁺ + 1.5 O₂ → Lys + CO₂ + 2 H₂O.
- ✅ **Glutamate stoichiometry (lumped):** Glc + NH₄⁺ + 0.5 O₂ → Glu + CO₂ + 1.5 H₂O.
- ✅ **NADPH demand:** 4 mol per mol Lys, 1 mol per mol Glu — supplied exclusively by oxidative PPP.
- ✅ **Minimum PPP split for lysine:** $v_{PPP}/v_{Glc}^{uptake} = 2/3 = 66.7\%$.
- ✅ **Theoretical max yields:** $Y^{\max}_{Lys/Glc} = 0.667$ mol/mol; $Y^{\max}_{Glu/Glc} = 1.0$ mol/mol.
- ✅ **Carbon yield efficiency:** $\sigma = (Y_{exp} / Y^{max}) \times 100\%$.
- ✅ **Anaplerotic flux via Pyc:** Essential for OAA supply to both pathways; 1 CO₂ fixed per Lys.
- ✅ **Industrial benchmarks:** Lys ~0.25–0.50 mol/mol (real); Glu ~0.6–0.8 mol/mol (real).
- ✅ **Key genetic interventions:**
  - *lysC* (aspartokinase, feedback-resistant) → ↑ Lys flux.
  - *gdh* overexpression → ↑ Glu flux.
  - *odhA* deletion (2-OG dehydrogenase) → ↑ Glu secretion.
  - *zwf*/*gnd* overexpression → ↑ NADPH supply.
  - *lysE* exporter engineering → ↑ Lys tolerance & secretion.
- ✅ **Notation conventions:** Use $v_i$ (mmol·gDW⁻¹·h⁻¹); positive for secretion, negative for uptake.
- ✅ **Mermaid safeguards:** alphanumeric prefixed IDs, no reserved keywords, no `|` in Markdown cells.
- ✅ **For Markdown tables:** never use the raw `|` character inside cells — use LaTeX `$\lvert$` or `$\mid$` instead.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fluxes in Mammalian Cell Cultures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT746 - METABOLIC ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fluxes in Mammalian Cell Cultures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🧬 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Metabolic Flux** in mammalian cell cultures refers to the *in vivo rate* at which metabolites are converted through the network of biochemical reactions inside a living mammalian cell population growing in a controlled bioreactor environment. It is quantified as the **specific metabolic rate** (mmol · 10⁹ cells⁻¹ · h⁻¹ or mmol · gDW⁻¹ · h⁻¹) at which each reaction proceeds under defined cultivation conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Standard**
> *Metabolic flux* is the **temporal rate of material flow through a defined intracellular reaction step**, calculated from extracellular metabolite balancing (mass balance over a stoichiometric matrix) under the assumption of a **pseudo-steady state (PSS)** for intracellular metabolites.

The discipline that quantifies these fluxes systematically is called **¹³C-Metabolic Flux Analysis (¹³C-MFA)** when using isotope tracers, or **Stoichiometric Flux Balance Analysis (FBA)** when based purely on mass balances.

## 1.2 The Conceptual Analogy — "The City's Traffic Map"

Imagine a **mammalian cell as a city** and its **metabolites as cars**:
- The **reactions (enzymes)** are the **roads** connecting intersections (metabolites).
- A **flux map** is the **real-time traffic heat-map** of the city — showing which roads are congested, which are empty, and where bottlenecks occur.
- **Glucose** is the *main highway entrance*, **lactate** is the *service road everyone takes when traffic is bad*, and **the TCA cycle** is the *underground expressway*.

> [!NOTE]
> Just as a city planner wants to redirect cars to reduce congestion and emissions, a metabolic engineer wants to *redistribute fluxes* to maximize **biomass** or **therapeutic protein (e.g., monoclonal antibody, mAb)** production while minimizing **toxic by-products** like lactate and ammonia.

## 1.3 Key Physiological Metrics in Mammalian Cell Cultures

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range (CHO cells) | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Specific glucose uptake rate | $q_{Glc}$ | 0.1 – 0.4 | mmol · 10⁹ cells⁻¹ · h⁻¹ |
| Specific glutamine uptake rate | $q_{Gln}$ | 0.03 – 0.10 | mmol · 10⁹ cells⁻¹ · h⁻¹ |
| Specific lactate production rate | $q_{Lac}$ | 0.2 – 0.6 | mmol · 10⁹ cells⁻¹ · h⁻¹ |
| Specific ammonia production rate | $q_{Amm}$ | 0.02 – 0.08 | mmol · 10⁹ cells⁻¹ · h⁻¹ |
| Specific growth rate | $\mu$ | 0.015 – 0.040 | h⁻¹ |
| Specific antibody productivity | $q_{mAb}$ | 20 – 100 | pg · cell⁻¹ · day⁻¹ |
| Cell viability (exponential phase) | — | > 95% | % |

> [!TIP]
> In mammalian cell culture, the **growth rate ($\mu$)** is *very low* (often 0.02 h⁻¹, doubling time ~35 h) compared to microbes ($E. coli$: $\mu \approx 0.7$ h⁻¹). This makes **flux measurements highly sensitive to small analytical errors**.

## 1.4 Why Mammalian Cells Behave Differently from Microbes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Mammalian cells (CHO, HEK293, NS0, hybridoma) exhibit **Overflow Metabolism** — they incompletely oxidize glucose to **lactate** even in the presence of abundant oxygen. This is the **Warburg-like effect** seen in rapidly proliferating cells, driven by high glycolytic enzyme expression, limited TCA cycle capacity, and high anabolic demand for *nucleotides, lipids, and amino acids*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mass Balance Framework for Flux Determination

The core principle is the **Metabolic Flux Analysis (MFA)** equation:

$$S \cdot \mathbf{v} = 0$$

Where:
- $S$ = **stoichiometric matrix** (size $m \times n$, where $m$ = metabolites, $n$ = reactions)
- $\mathbf{v}$ = **flux vector** (the unknown intracellular rates we want to find)
- The equation $\mathbf{0}$ represents the **pseudo-steady-state assumption** for all intracellular pools

### Why "pseudo-steady state"?

> [!NOTE]
> Because intracellular metabolite concentrations (ATP, NADH, citrate, etc.) change *much faster* (seconds to minutes) than the cell density, substrate, or product concentrations (hours to days), we assume these intermediate pools are **at steady state**, allowing the linear algebra solution of $S \cdot \mathbf{v} = 0$.

## 2.2 The Six Logical Steps of Flux Analysis in Mammalian Cells

1. **Define the metabolic network** — include glycolysis, TCA cycle, pentose phosphate pathway (PPP), glutaminolysis, amino acid metabolism, biomass synthesis, maintenance ATP, and product formation.
2. **Construct the stoichiometric matrix $S$** — one row per internal metabolite, one column per reaction.
3. **Measure extracellular fluxes** — calculate $q_i$ values from time-course data of substrates and products in the bioreactor.
4. **Apply the pseudo-steady-state constraint** — $S \cdot \mathbf{v} = 0$ for all internal metabolites.
5. **Solve the linear system** — using least-squares if over-determined (more equations than unknowns) or linear programming if under-determined.
6. **Validate** — compare predicted vs measured ¹³C-labeling patterns (for ¹³C-MFA) or use parsimonious FBA (pFBA) to find the most biologically realistic flux distribution.

## 2.3 Specific Rate Calculation (The Foundation)

The most fundamental experimental measurement is the **specific consumption/production rate** $q_i$:

$$q_i = \frac{1}{X} \cdot \frac{dC_i}{dt}$$

Where:
- $X$ = viable cell density (cells/mL or 10⁶ cells/mL)
- $C_i$ = concentration of metabolite $i$ (mM)
- $t$ = time (h)

> [!TIP]
> For fed-batch and batch cultures, $q_i$ is calculated as the **slope of $C_i$ vs $t$** divided by the **average $X$** over that interval. This is the **single most important experimental quantity** in KTU 2024 numerical problems on this topic.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $S \cdot \mathbf{v} = 0$ | Mass balance at pseudo-steady state | Dimensionless × rate |
| 2 | $q_i = \dfrac{1}{X} \cdot \dfrac{dC_i}{dt}$ | Specific rate of metabolite $i$ | mmol · cell⁻¹ · h⁻¹ |
| 3 | $\mu = \dfrac{1}{X} \cdot \dfrac{dX}{dt}$ | Specific growth rate | h⁻¹ |
| 4 | $Y_{X/S} = \dfrac{\Delta X}{-\Delta S}$ | Biomass yield on substrate $S$ | cells · mmol⁻¹ or gDW · g⁻¹ |
| 5 | $r_{ATP,maint} = m_{ATP} \cdot X$ | Maintenance ATP consumption | mmol · L⁻¹ · h⁻¹ |
| 6 | $v_{biomass} = \mu \cdot X / Y_{X}^{max}$ | Biomass synthesis flux | mmol · L⁻¹ · h⁻¹ |
| 7 | $q_{CO_2} = a \cdot q_{Glc} + b \cdot q_{Gln}$ | CO₂ production (linear combination) | mmol · cell⁻¹ · h⁻¹ |
| 8 | $r_i = \mu \cdot c_{i,biomass} + \dots$ | Flux into biomass for component $i$ | mmol · gDW⁻¹ · h⁻¹ |
| 9 | $\Delta G_{ATP}^{maint} = m_{ATP} \cdot X \cdot V$ | Maintenance energy rate | mmol · L⁻¹ · h⁻¹ |
| 10 | $\dfrac{dC_{Lac}}{dt} = q_{Lac} \cdot X$ | Lactate accumulation rate | mM · h⁻¹ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Units Trap** — In KTU numericals, students often mix up $q_i$ (per cell basis) and $r_i$ (per volume basis). They are related by $r_i = q_i \cdot X$.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding fluxes in mammalian cell cultures is the cornerstone of:

- **Process optimization** for mAb, vaccine, and recombinant protein production (e.g., trastuzumab, rituximab)
- **Cell line engineering** — knocking out lactate dehydrogenase (LDHA) to suppress lactate overflow
- **Media design** — replacing glucose with galactose or switching to glutamine-free media
- **Scale-up** — from shake flasks (50 mL) to 20,000 L bioreactors for industrial biologics manufacturing
- **Quality-by-Design (QbD)** — controlling critical quality attributes (glycosylation) via metabolic flux redirection

## 2.6 The Three Major Carbon Sources in Mammalian Metabolism

```
Glucose    →  Glycolysis      →  Pyruvate  →  Lactate (overflow) OR  Acetyl-CoA  →  TCA
Glutamine  →  Glutaminolysis  →  α-KG      →  TCA cycle intermediates (anaplerosis)
Other AAs  →  Transaminations →  TCA + biosynthesis
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Glutamine** is the **second most important carbon source** in mammalian cells — often consuming as much as 30–60% of the energy budget. The enzyme **glutaminase (GLS)** converts glutamine to glutamate, fueling the TCA cycle via **α-ketoglutarate**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Specific Rate from Time-Course Data

**Problem Setup:** In a CHO cell batch culture, viable cell density $X$ increases from $1.0 \times 10^6$ cells/mL to $2.5 \times 10^6$ cells/mL in 24 hours, while glucose drops from 25 mM to 18 mM. Calculate $\mu$ and $q_{Glc}$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Identify the governing equation.

$$\mu = \frac{1}{X} \cdot \frac{dX}{dt}$$

Since $X$ changes significantly, we use the **logarithmic form** (more accurate for exponential growth):

$$\mu = \frac{\ln(X_2 / X_1)}{t_2 - t_1}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the values.

$$\mu = \frac{\ln\left(\dfrac{2.5 \times 10^6}{1.0 \times 10^6}\right)}{24 - 0} = \frac{\ln(2.5)}{24}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the natural logarithm.

$$\ln(2.5) = 0.9163$$

**Step 4:** Compute $\mu$.

$$\mu = \frac{0.9163}{24} = 0.0382 \ \text{h}^{-1}$$

**Step 5:** Identify the governing equation for $q_{Glc}$.

$$q_{Glc} = \frac{1}{\bar{X}} \cdot \frac{\Delta C_{Glc}}{\Delta t}$$

Where $\bar{X}$ is the average cell density:

$$\bar{X} = \frac{X_2 - X_1}{\ln(X_2 / X_1)} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^6 - 1.0 \times 10^6}{0.9163}$$

**Step 6:** Compute $\bar{X}$.

$$\bar{X} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^6}{0.9163} = 1.637 \times 10^6 \ \text{cells/mL}$$

**Step 7:** Compute $\Delta C_{Glc} / \Delta t$.

$$\frac{\Delta C_{Glc}}{\Delta t} = \frac{18 - 25}{24} = \frac{-7}{24} = -0.2917 \ \text{mM/h}$$

The negative sign indicates glucose *consumption*.

**Step 8:** Compute $q_{Glc}$.

$$q_{Glc} = \frac{-0.2917}{1.637 \times 10^6} = -1.782 \times 10^{-7} \ \text{mmol/cell/h}$$

**Step 9:** Convert to KTU-preferred units of mmol · 10⁹ cells⁻¹ · h⁻¹.

$$q_{Glc} = -1.782 \times 10^{-7} \times 10^9 = -0.178 \ \text{mmol} \cdot 10^9 \ \text{cells}^{-1} \cdot \text{h}^{-1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The negative sign is **always retained** in $q$ notation to indicate *net uptake*. A positive sign would indicate *net production* (e.g., lactate).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — The Mass Balance Equation for Lactate

**Problem Setup:** A CHO culture has $X = 3.0 \times 10^6$ cells/mL and produces lactate at $q_{Lac} = 0.30$ mmol · 10⁹ cells⁻¹ · h⁻¹. The reactor volume is 2 L. What is the lactate accumulation rate per liter?

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** State the volumetric rate equation.

$$r_{Lac} = q_{Lac} \cdot X$$

**Step 2:** Convert $q_{Lac}$ to consistent units.

$$q_{Lac} = 0.30 \ \text{mmol} \cdot 10^9 \ \text{cells}^{-1} \cdot \text{h}^{-1} = 3.0 \times 10^{-10} \ \text{mmol} \cdot \text{cell}^{-1} \cdot \text{h}^{-1}$$

**Step 3:** Multiply by cell density.

$$r_{Lac} = 3.0 \times 10^{-10} \times 3.0 \times 10^6 = 9.0 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{mmol} \cdot \text{mL}^{-1} \cdot \text{h}^{-1}$$

**Step 4:** Convert to mM/h (since 1 mmol/mL = 1000 mM).

$$r_{Lac} = 9.0 \times 10^{-4} \times 1000 = 0.90 \ \text{mM/h}$$

**Step 5:** State the final accumulated concentration in 2 L.

$$\Delta C_{Lac} \text{ in 2 L} = 0.90 \ \text{mM/h (per liter basis — same in 2 L)}$$

In total moles: $0.90 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{mol/L} \times 2 \ \text{L} = 1.8 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{mol in 2 L per hour}$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Solving a Simple Stoichiometric Flux Model

**Problem Setup:** Consider a simplified 3-reaction network:
- **R1:** Glucose → 2 Pyruvate (glycolysis)
- **R2:** Pyruvate → Lactate (LDH)
- **R3:** Pyruvate → Acetyl-CoA + CO₂ (PDH)

**Reaction stoichiometric matrix $S$ (rows = metabolites, columns = reactions):**

| Metabolite \ Reaction | R1 | R2 | R3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Glucose | -1 | 0 | 0 |
| Pyruvate | +2 | -1 | -1 |
| Lactate | 0 | +1 | 0 |
| Acetyl-CoA | 0 | 0 | +1 |
| CO₂ | 0 | 0 | +1 |

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Write the matrix equation $S \cdot v = 0$.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Glucose:} \quad & -v_1 = 0 \Rightarrow v_1 = v_{Glc,uptake} \\
\text{Pyruvate:} \quad & 2v_1 - v_2 - v_3 = 0 \\
\text{Lactate:} \quad & v_2 = v_{Lac,prod} \\
\text{Acetyl-CoA:} \quad & v_3 = v_{AcCoA} \\
\text{CO}_2\text{:} \quad & v_3 = v_{CO_2,prod}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Recognize that $v_2$ and $v_3$ are the **measured/balanced** outputs of glycolysis.

From the **pyruvate balance**:
$$2v_1 = v_2 + v_3$$

**Step 3:** If we experimentally measure $v_2 = 0.5$ mmol/gDW/h (lactate) and $v_3 = 0.3$ mmol/gDW/h (PDH), then:

$$v_1 = \frac{v_2 + v_3}{2} = \frac{0.5 + 0.3}{2} = 0.4 \ \text{mmol/gDW/h}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the **flux split ratio** (lactate vs. PDH):

$$\text{LDH flux \%} = \frac{v_2}{v_2 + v_3} \times 100\% = \frac{0.5}{0.8} \times 100\% = 62.5\%$$

$$\text{PDH flux \%} = \frac{v_3}{v_2 + v_3} \times 100\% = \frac{0.3}{0.8} \times 100\% = 37.5\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This **flux split ratio** is a **key engineering lever**. By overexpressing PDH or knocking out LDHA, the engineer can shift the split from 62.5/37.5 toward 20/80, dramatically reducing lactate accumulation in the bioreactor.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — The Biomass Synthesis Equation (Hereditary)

A generalized **biomass formation flux** in a mammalian cell is given by:

$$v_{biomass} = \frac{\mu}{Y_X^{max}} \cdot X$$

Where $Y_X^{max}$ is the maximum theoretical yield of biomass on the limiting substrate.

For CHO cells with **glucose as limiting substrate**:

$$Y_{X/Glc}^{max} = \frac{1}{\sigma_{Glc,biomass}}$$

Where $\sigma_{Glc,biomass}$ is the **stoichiometric coefficient of glucose** in the empirical biomass formula:

$$\text{CHO biomass} \approx \text{CH}_{1.86}\text{O}_{0.62}\text{N}_{0.16}$$

The overall **biomass synthesis reaction** (with the empirical formula) is:

$$\text{a·Glc} + \text{b·Gln} + \text{c·Other AAs} + \text{d·O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_{1.86}\text{O}_{0.62}\text{N}_{0.16} + \text{e·CO}_2 + \text{f·H}_2\text{O} + \text{g·Lactate} + \text{h·NH}_3$$

The coefficients (a, b, c, …) are determined by **elemental balancing** (C, H, O, N, and degree-of-reduction balance).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Common Numerical Scenarios in KTU Exams

| Scenario | Governing Equations | Key Assumption |
|---|---|---|
| Calculate $q_{Glc}$ from time-course | $q_{Glc} = -\dfrac{1}{\bar{X}} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta C_{Glc}}{\Delta t}$ | Pseudo-steady state for $X$ |
| Determine biomass yield | $Y_{X/S} = \dfrac{\Delta X}{-\Delta S}$ | Single limiting substrate |
| Solve stoichiometric network | $S \cdot v = 0$ with measured $v$'s | PSS for internal metabolites |
| ATP maintenance calculation | $r_{ATP,maint} = m_{ATP} \cdot X$ | $m_{ATP}$ ≈ 0.5 mmol/gDW/h for CHO |
| Fed-batch glucose feed design | $\dfrac{dC_{Glc}}{dt} = 0 = F_{in} \cdot C_{feed} - q_{Glc} \cdot X - \dots$ | Glucose set-point maintained |
| ¹³C-MFA flux precision | $\chi^2$ minimization vs. measured MS data | Atom transitions from labeled substrates |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

> **Reading the Flux Map:** In a flux heatmap, *red* = high flux (active pathway), *blue* = low flux (dormant pathway), and *white* = no flux (impossible reaction). Most CHO flux maps show **bright red** in the glycolysis-to-lactate arm in early exponential phase.

# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
**Q:** Define the term "metabolic flux" and explain why the *pseudo-steady-state* assumption is essential for intracellular metabolites in flux analysis.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Definition (1.5 marks):** Metabolic flux is the in vivo rate (mmol · gDW⁻¹ · h⁻¹) at which a metabolic reaction proceeds within a cell. It represents the rate of material flow through a defined pathway step.
2. **Pseudo-steady-state (1.5 marks):** Intracellular metabolite pools (ATP, NADH, intermediates) turn over in seconds, much faster than the timescale of cell growth or substrate depletion (hours). Thus, their net accumulation $dC_i/dt \approx 0$, allowing the linear mass-balance equation $S \cdot v = 0$ to be applied.

---

### Question A.2
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
**Q:** What is **overflow metabolism**, and why is it particularly problematic in industrial CHO cell cultures used for mAb production?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Definition (1.5 marks):** Overflow metabolism is the phenomenon where cells incompletely oxidize substrates (e.g., glucose → lactate) despite oxygen availability, wasting carbon as reduced by-products.
2. **Why problematic in CHO (1.5 marks):** It causes lactate accumulation, leading to medium acidification (pH drop), osmotic stress, reduced cell viability, lower viable cell density, and inhibition of product synthesis. It also wastes carbon that could otherwise be used for biomass or mAb formation.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark ESE Question with Internal Choice

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme – Module 2]`**

**(a)** A CHO cell fed-batch culture has the following data over 24 hours:

| Time (h) | Viable Cells (10⁶/mL) | Glucose (mM) | Lactate (mM) | Glutamine (mM) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 1.0 | 30 | 5 | 4.0 |
| 24 | 4.0 | 18 | 22 | 2.5 |

Calculate:
- (i) The specific growth rate $\mu$ (2 marks)
- (ii) The specific glucose uptake rate $q_{Glc}$ (2 marks)
- (iii) The specific lactate production rate $q_{Lac}$ (2 marks)
- (iv) The yield coefficient $Y_{Lac/Glc}$ (1 mark)

**(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the calculated $q_{Glc}$ and $q_{Lac}$ values from part (a), set up and solve the simplified stoichiometric model (Glucose → 2 Pyruvate; Pyruvate → Lactate; Pyruvate → Acetyl-CoA + CO₂). Comment on the **flux split ratio** and suggest **two engineering strategies** to reduce lactate accumulation.

**(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Step-by-Step Calculation:**

**(i) Specific growth rate $\mu$:**
Using logarithmic mean: $\mu = \dfrac{\ln(4.0/1.0)}{24} = \dfrac{\ln 4}{24} = \dfrac{1.3863}{24} = 0.0578 \ \text{h}^{-1}$

**(ii) Specific glucose uptake rate $q_{Glc}$:**
Average cell density: $\bar{X} = \dfrac{4.0 - 1.0}{\ln(4.0/1.0)} = \dfrac{3.0}{1.3863} = 2.164 \times 10^6 \ \text{cells/mL}$
$\Delta C_{Glc}/24 = (18 - 30)/24 = -0.5 \ \text{mM/h}$
$q_{Glc} = -0.5 / (2.164 \times 10^6) = -2.31 \times 10^{-7} \ \text{mmol/cell/h} = -0.231 \ \text{mmol} \cdot 10^9 \ \text{cells}^{-1} \cdot \text{h}^{-1}$

**(iii) Specific lactate production rate $q_{Lac}$:**
$\Delta C_{Lac}/24 = (22 - 5)/24 = +0.708 \ \text{mM/h}$
$q_{Lac} = 0.708 / (2.164 \times 10^6) = 3.27 \times 10^{-7} \ \text{mmol/cell/h} = 0.327 \ \text{mmol} \cdot 10^9 \ \text{cells}^{-1} \cdot \text{h}^{-1}$

**(iv) Yield $Y_{Lac/Glc}$:**
$Y_{Lac/Glc} = \Delta C_{Lac} / (-\Delta C_{Glc}) = 17 / 12 = 1.417 \ \text{mol/mol}$

> [!NOTE]
> A $Y_{Lac/Glc}$ > 1.0 indicates that **other carbon sources (glutamine, amino acids) are also contributing to lactate production** — a common signature of overflow metabolism in CHO cells.

**Part (b) — Model Setup and Solution:**

Network reactions:
- **R1:** Glucose → 2 Pyruvate ($v_1$)
- **R2:** Pyruvate → Lactate ($v_2$)
- **R3:** Pyruvate → Acetyl-CoA + CO₂ ($v_3$)

Measured/derived rates (per cell basis):
- $v_1 = q_{Glc} = 0.231$ (mmol · 10⁹ cells⁻¹ · h⁻¹)
- $v_2 = q_{Lac} = 0.327$ (mmol · 10⁹ cells⁻¹ · h⁻¹)

Pyruvate balance: $2v_1 = v_2 + v_3$
$2(0.231) = 0.327 + v_3$
$v_3 = 0.462 - 0.327 = 0.135$ (mmol · 10⁹ cells⁻¹ · h⁻¹)

**Flux split ratio:**
- LDH fraction: $0.327 / 0.462 = 70.8\%$
- PDH fraction: $0.135 / 0.462 = 29.2\%$

**Two Engineering Strategies to Reduce Lactate:**
1. **Cell Line Engineering:** Use CRISPR-Cas9 or siRNA to knock out **LDHA** (lactate dehydrogenase A) gene, redirecting pyruvate toward mitochondrial oxidation via PDH.
2. **Media Design / Feeding Strategy:** Replace glucose partially with **galactose** (slower glycolysis), or implement a *temperature shift* (from 37°C to 32–33°C) to lower $q_{Glc}$ and reduce overflow.

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme – Module 2]`**

**(a)** Derive the mass balance equation $S \cdot v = 0$ starting from the general mole balance for a metabolite $i$ in a continuous stirred-tank bioreactor (CSTR). Clearly state each term. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A monoclonal antibody-producing CHO cell line in a chemostat at $D = 0.02$ h⁻¹ has the following steady-state data:
- $S_{Glc,in} = 25$ mM, $S_{Glc,out} = 5$ mM
- $S_{Gln,in} = 6$ mM, $S_{Gln,out} = 1$ mM
- $X = 2.0 \times 10^6$ cells/mL
- $P_{Lac,out} = 18$ mM, $P_{NH_3,out} = 2.5$ mM

Calculate $q_{Glc}$, $q_{Gln}$, $q_{Lac}$, and $q_{NH_3}$ in mmol · 10⁹ cells⁻¹ · h⁻¹, and comment on the metabolic state of the culture. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

For a CSTR at steady state, the mole balance on metabolite $i$ in the **intracellular** pool is:

$$0 = \sum_{j} S_{ij} v_j$$

Derivation steps:
1. **General balance:** $\dfrac{dn_i}{dt} = F_{in}C_{i,in} - F_{out}C_{i,out} + \sum_j S_{ij} v_j \cdot V + r_{i,accumul}$
2. **Intracellular pool** has no inlet/outlet in the traditional sense — the cell membrane is the boundary: $\dfrac{dn_{i,intra}}{dt} = 0 + \sum_j S_{ij} v_j$ (no feed streams of intermediates)
3. **Pseudo-steady-state assumption:** $\dfrac{dn_{i,intra}}{dt} \approx 0$ because internal pool turnover is much faster than growth.
4. **Therefore:** $\sum_j S_{ij} v_j = 0$
5. **Vector form:** $S \cdot v = 0$, where $S$ is the $m \times n$ stoichiometric matrix and $v$ is the $n \times 1$ flux vector.

**Part (b) — Chemostat Calculations:**

For a CSTR at steady state, $q_i = D \cdot (C_{i,in} - C_{i,out}) / X$.

Dilution rate: $D = F_{in}/V = 0.02$ h⁻¹.

**$q_{Glc}$:**
$q_{Glc} = D \cdot (S_{Glc,in} - S_{Glc,out}) / X = 0.02 \times (25 - 5) / (2.0 \times 10^6) = 0.02 \times 20 / (2.0 \times 10^6) = 2.0 \times 10^{-7}$ mmol/cell/h = **0.20 mmol · 10⁹ cells⁻¹ · h⁻¹** (consumption)

**$q_{Gln}$:**
$q_{Gln} = 0.02 \times (6 - 1) / (2.0 \times 10^6) = 0.02 \times 5 / (2.0 \times 10^6) = 5.0 \times 10^{-8}$ = **0.05 mmol · 10⁹ cells⁻¹ · h⁻¹** (consumption)

**$q_{Lac}$:**
$q_{Lac} = 0.02 \times (18 - 0) / (2.0 \times 10^6) = 1.8 \times 10^{-7}$ = **0.18 mmol · 10⁹ cells⁻¹ · h⁻¹** (production)

**$q_{NH_3}$:**
$q_{NH_3} = 0.02 \times (2.5 - 0) / (2.0 \times 10^6) = 2.5 \times 10^{-8}$ = **0.025 mmol · 10⁹ cells⁻¹ · h⁻¹** (production)

**Comment on metabolic state:**
The high $q_{Lac}/q_{Glc} = 0.18/0.20 = 0.9$ indicates **strong overflow metabolism** (90% of consumed glucose is converted to lactate). The ratio $q_{Gln}/q_{Glc} = 0.25$ shows that glutamine is a significant secondary carbon/energy source. Elevated $q_{NH_3}$ suggests active **glutaminolysis** releasing ammonium.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the sign convention** in $q$ values: uptake is *negative*, production is *positive* in vector notation, but the KTU key often expects *magnitudes with verbal labels* ("consumed" vs "produced").
> 2. **Mixing units** between mmol/L (concentration) and mmol · 10⁹ cells⁻¹ · h⁻¹ (specific rate). Always state both unit conversions explicitly.
> 3. **Using linear interpolation** of $X$ instead of the **logarithmic mean** for specific rate calculations in exponential growth phases. This causes a 5–15% systematic error.
> 4. **Ignoring the volume change** in fed-batch mode — the dilution effect from feeding alters all concentration measurements and must be accounted for.
> 5. **Confusing $S \cdot v = 0$ with $S \cdot v = r$** (the former is for *intracellular* PSS; the latter includes measured net accumulation rates for *extracellular* metabolites).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### ⚡ Rapid-Revision Checklist

- ✅ **Definition:** Flux = rate of material flow through a reaction (mmol · gDW⁻¹ · h⁻¹ or mmol · 10⁹ cells⁻¹ · h⁻¹)
- ✅ **Core Equation:** $S \cdot v = 0$ (stoichiometric matrix × flux vector = zero at pseudo-steady state)
- ✅ **Specific Rate Formula:** $q_i = \dfrac{1}{X} \cdot \dfrac{dC_i}{dt}$ (always use $\bar{X}$ as logarithmic mean in exponential growth)
- ✅ **Mammalian Signature:** Overflow metabolism → glucose → lactate (Warburg-like effect)
- ✅ **Two Major Fuels:** Glucose (glycolysis → lactate/acetyl-CoA) + Glutamine (glutaminolysis → α-KG → TCA)
- ✅ **Pseudo-Steady State:** Internal metabolite pools are assumed to have $dC_i/dt \approx 0$ due to fast turnover
- ✅ **Biomass Yield:** $Y_{X/S} = \Delta X / (-\Delta S)$; typical CHO $Y_{X/Glc} \approx 0.2$ gDW/g
- ✅ **Maintenance ATP:** $r_{ATP,maint} = m_{ATP} \cdot X$ where $m_{ATP} \approx 0.5$ mmol/gDW/h for CHO
- ✅ **Flux Split Ratios:** LDH vs PDH partitioning of pyruvate is a key engineering target
- ✅ **CHO Cell Values to Memorize:** $q_{Glc}$ ≈ 0.1–0.4, $q_{Lac}$ ≈ 0.2–0.6, $\mu$ ≈ 0.02–0.04 h⁻¹
- ✅ **Difference from Microbes:** Mammalian cells are slow-growing, more delicate, exhibit overflow, and have complex glycosylation requirements
- ✅ **Engineering Levers:** LDHA knockout, glucose-galactose switch, temperature shift, glutamine substitution, dynamic feeding
- ✅ **Industrial Relevance:** Biologics manufacturing (mAbs, vaccines, recombinant proteins) — multi-billion dollar market
- ✅ **Software Tools:** OpenFlux, INCA, 13CFLUX2 — used for ¹³C-MFA in mammalian systems
- ✅ **Validation Step:** Always cross-check computed fluxes with ¹³C-labeling data or independent measurements (e.g., oxygen uptake rate, OUR)

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip:** In KTU 2024 ESE, questions on this topic almost always combine a **numerical specific-rate calculation** (3–5 marks) with a **theoretical question** on metabolic engineering intervention (3–5 marks) in a single 7-mark sub-part. Practice both skills together.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flux Analysis and Design of Culture Media
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT746 - METABOLIC ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Flux Analysis and Design of Culture Media

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Flux Analysis — Formal Definition

**Metabolic Flux Analysis (MFA)** is the systematic quantification of the rates (fluxes) of biochemical reactions through a metabolic network under steady-state conditions. It is the quantitative backbone of metabolic engineering, providing a snapshot of how carbon, nitrogen, and energy flow through the cell's enzymatic machinery.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Flux Analysis is the determination of intracellular metabolic fluxes using stoichiometric models combined with measured extracellular rates (substrate uptake, product formation, biomass accumulation), assuming pseudo-steady-state for intracellular metabolites.*

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The City Traffic Analogy"

Imagine a city's road network. Roads are **reactions**, intersections are **metabolites**, and cars represent **carbon atoms**. You have sensors at the city boundaries (glucose going in, CO₂ and ethanol coming out). You want to know how many cars travel on *each* road. You can't put a camera on every intersection (that would be measuring every internal metabolite), but because the total number of cars entering equals those leaving at steady state (no cars pile up), you can **mathematically deduce** the flow on internal roads.

This is exactly what flux analysis does:
- **Boundary sensors** → Measured extracellular fluxes
- **Road network** → Stoichiometric matrix (S)
- **Steady-state assumption** → S · v = 0
- **Deducing internal flows** → Linear system solving / optimization

## 1.3 Design of Culture Media — Formal Definition

**Culture Media Design** is the rational formulation of a nutrient mixture that supplies all elements (C, H, N, O, S, P, trace metals, vitamins) required for biomass synthesis, product formation, and energy generation in a controlled bioprocess. The goal is to achieve a **C:N:P ratio** that maximizes the yield coefficient (Y) of the desired product while minimizing by-product formation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric in Media Design:** The **Biomass Yield Coefficient** $Y_{X/S}$ (g biomass per g substrate consumed) is the primary design response variable.

## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — "The Recipe Analogy"

Designing a culture medium is like baking a cake. Too much sugar → burnt edges (overflow metabolism, e.g., acetate in *E. coli*). Too little flour → collapsed structure (poor growth). The medium is a **balanced recipe** where every ingredient (glucose, ammonium, phosphate, magnesium, trace elements) must be in the right proportion to support the **"rising"** of biomass and the desired **"flavor"** (product).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Foundations of Flux Analysis

### 2.1.1 The Stoichiometric Matrix (S)

Every metabolic network of **m** metabolites and **n** reactions can be encoded as a matrix **S** of dimensions *m × n*. Each element $S_{ij}$ represents the stoichiometric coefficient of metabolite *i* in reaction *j* (negative for substrates, positive for products).

### 2.1.2 Mass Balance Equation (Cornerstone of MFA)

At **pseudo-steady state** for intracellular metabolites:

$$\frac{dC_i}{dt} = 0 = \sum_{j=1}^{n} S_{ij} \cdot v_j$$

Where:
- $C_i$ = intracellular concentration of metabolite *i*
- $v_j$ = flux (rate) of reaction *j* in mmol gDW⁻¹ h⁻¹

This yields the **compact matrix form**:

$$S \cdot v = 0$$

### 2.1.3 Degrees of Freedom (DoF)

The system is **underdetermined** (more reactions than metabolites). The number of **free fluxes** (DoF) that must be measured experimentally is:

$$DoF = n - \text{rank}(S)$$

For a typical *E. coli* central carbon metabolism: ~30 reactions, rank(S) ≈ 25, DoF ≈ 5. So we measure 5 extracellular fluxes to uniquely determine all internal fluxes.

### 2.1.4 Flux Balance Analysis (FBA)

When DoF exceeds the number of easily measured fluxes, **FBA** solves the system by **optimizing** an objective function (typically biomass maximization):
