- ☐ **Branch ratio of FCCs:** $\dfrac{C^{J_A}_{e_0}}{C^{J_B}_{e_0}} = -\dfrac{\varepsilon^{v_B}_{S_b}}{\varepsilon^{v_A}_{S_b}}$.
- ☐ **Negative FCCs are physical:** Mean increasing that enzyme *decreases* flux (due to redistribution of metabolites).
- ☐ **Engineering implication:** Target enzymes with $C^J \to 1$ for maximum flux gain.
- ☐ **Top-down vs bottom-up MCA:** Top-down groups enzymes (e.g., into blocks); bottom-up solves enzyme by enzyme.
- ☐ **MCA limitation:** Requires steady state, small perturbations, and known elasticities — impractical for genome-scale models (use FBA or regulatory FBA instead).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Functional Genomics, Proteomics, Metabolomics, Systems Biology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBBT746 - METABOLIC ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Functional Genomics, Proteomics, Metabolomics, Systems Biology ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Functional Genomics — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Functional Genomics** is the discipline that systematically deciphers the *dynamic expression, interaction, regulation, and function* of genes and their products across an entire genome, using high-throughput experimental and computational techniques, rather than studying single genes in isolation (as in classical genetics). It moves beyond the static mapping of the genome (structural genomics) to ask the question: **"What does each gene do, when does it do it, and with whom does it cooperate?"** **Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):** Imagine a vast city (the genome) where every house has a name plate (gene sequence). Structural genomics gave us the addresses. Functional genomics is the *CCTV control room* that observes which houses turn on lights at dawn, which are dark, which fire stations respond together, and which streets are jammed during rush hour. It correlates activity with *purpose* — telling us, for instance, that Gene X spikes only when glucose is low, meaning it likely codes for a starvation-response enzyme. > [!NOTE] > **Key Sub-domains of Functional Genomics:** > - **Transcriptomics** — global mRNA profiling (RNA-Seq, microarrays) > - **Comparative Genomics** — function inferred by homology > - **Forward/Reverse Genetics** — phenotypic screening of knockouts/overexpression > - **Epigenomics** — heritable changes not in the DNA sequence (methylation, histone marks) --- ### 1.2 Proteomics — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Proteomics** is the large-scale, systematic study of the entire set of proteins (the **proteome**) expressed by a cell, tissue, or organism at a given time, including their structures, abundances, modifications, interactions, and functions. A single human gene can produce **multiple protein isoforms** via alternative splicing and **>200 post-translational modifications (PTMs)** such as phosphorylation, glycosylation, and acetylation — features invisible at the mRNA level. **Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):** If the genome is a *cookbook*, then: - **Transcriptomics** is the list of recipes copied from the cookbook (mRNA). - **Proteomics** is the *actual prepared dishes on the table* — including garnishes, plating, temperature, and what has been partially eaten. Just because a recipe is copied doesn't mean the dish is cooked, and even if cooked, the dish may be modified (e.g., extra spice = PTM) before serving. > [!NOTE] > **Categories of Proteomics:** > - **Expression Proteomics** — quantitative profiling of protein abundance > - **Structural Proteomics** — 3-D architecture of every protein > - **Interaction Proteomics** — protein–protein interaction (PPI) networks > - **Functional Proteomics** — signaling cascades, enzymatic activity profiling --- ### 1.3 Metabolomics — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Metabolomics** is the comprehensive qualitative and quantitative analysis of the complete set of small-molecule metabolites (the **metabolome**, typically **<1500 Da**) present in a biological system, providing a direct snapshot of its biochemical/physiological state. **Standard Metrics for Metabolomics:** - **Number of endogenous metabolites in central carbon metabolism:** ~**50–150** in microbes - **Total plant metabolome size:** ~**200,000** distinct compounds - **Detection limit of modern LC-MS:** **pmol/L to nmol/L** range - **Human serum metabolome:** ~**4,000** quantified metabolites **Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):** Genes and proteins are the *plan and the workers*. The metabolome is the *product coming off the assembly line* — and the *factory's power consumption, waste, and exhaust* combined. It is the **closest molecular readout of phenotype**: the cell's final, integrated response to its environment. > [!NOTE] > **Two Major Branches:** > - **Targeted Metabolomics** — quantifies a predefined set (~50–500 metabolites) with high accuracy; uses **MRM (Multiple Reaction Monitoring)** in triple-quadrupole MS. > - **Untargeted Metabolomics** — unbiased survey of all detectable signals; relies on **GC-MS, LC-MS, or NMR** and statistical deconvolution. --- ### 1.4 Systems Biology — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Systems Biology** is the integrative, holistic study of biological systems in which all components (genes, mRNAs, proteins, metabolites) and their interactions are examined simultaneously as a **mathematically modelled, dynamic network**, rather than as isolated parts. It aims to understand emergent properties that no single component exhibits alone. **Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):** Classical biology is like studying one musician. Systems biology is studying the **entire orchestra** — looking at the score, the conductor, the acoustics of the hall, and how the music *emerges* from synchrony. You cannot understand a symphony by isolating a violin. The four pillars of systems biology are: 1. **Multi-omics data integration** 2. **Mathematical/computational modelling** 3. **Iterative model–experiment cycles** 4. **Emergent behaviour discovery** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Distinction (Frequently tested in KTU):** > Systems biology is **NOT** the same as bioinformatics. Bioinformatics *manages* biological data; systems biology *integrates* it with **mechanistic, predictive models**. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The "Omics Cascade" — Conceptual Flow Every biological information flow follows the **Central Dogma extended to function**: $$ \text{Genome} \xrightarrow{\text{Transcription}} \text{Transcriptome} \xrightarrow{\text{Translation}} \text{Proteome} \xrightarrow{\text{Catalysis/Signalling}} \text{Metabolome} \xrightarrow{} \text{Phenotype} $$ - **Functional Genomics** interrogates the Genome → Transcriptome link. - **Proteomics** interrogates the Transcriptome → Proteome link (and beyond). - **Metabolomics** interrogates the Proteome → Metabolome link. - **Systems Biology** integrates ALL of them simultaneously. --- ### 2.2 Functional Genomics — Theoretical Layers **Why it matters in metabolic engineering:** - Identifies **rate-limiting enzymes** via expression profiling under different carbon sources. - Reveals **co-regulated gene clusters (operons, regulons)** to predict synergistic knock-in/knock-out targets. - Detects **silent gene clusters** that may be activated for novel product biosynthesis. **Key Methodologies:** | Technique | What it Measures | Throughput | Cost | |---|---|---|---| | **RNA-Seq** | mRNA abundance (digital) | High (10⁹ reads) | Moderate | | **Microarray** | mRNA abundance (analog) | High | Low | | **ChIP-Seq** | Transcription factor binding sites | High | Moderate | | **ATAC-Seq** | Chromatin accessibility | High | Moderate | | **CRISPRi/a screening** | Gene function via knockdown/activation | Genome-scale | High | --- ### 2.3 Proteomics — Theoretical Layers **Mass Spectrometry Workflow (most common):** $$ \text{Protein Mixture} \xrightarrow{\text{Digestion (Trypsin)}} \text{Peptides} \xrightarrow{\text{LC Separation}} \text{Ionization (ESI/MALDI)} \xrightarrow{\text{MS/MS}} \text{Spectrum} \xrightarrow{\text{Database Search}} \text{Identifications} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Two Ionization Methods — KTU Favourite:** > - **ESI (Electrospray Ionization)** — soft, ideal for intact proteins and on-line LC coupling. Produces multiply charged ions. > - **MALDI (Matrix-Assisted Laser Desorption/Ionization)** — pulsed, ideal for high-throughput peptide mass fingerprinting. Produces singly charged ions. **Yeast Two-Hybrid (Y2H) for PPI:** - **Bait** protein fused to DNA-Binding Domain (DBD). - **Prey** protein fused to Activation Domain (AD). - Interaction reconstitutes functional transcription factor → reporter gene (e.g., *HIS3*, *LacZ*) is expressed. - Limitation: high false-positive rate (~**30–50%**), cannot detect membrane proteins well. --- ### 2.4 Metabolomics — Theoretical Layers **Steady-state mass balance inside a cell** (the foundation of all metabolic modelling): $$ \frac{dC_i}{dt} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} S_{ij} \cdot v_j - \mu \cdot C_i $$ Where: - $C_i$ = concentration of metabolite *i* (mM) - $S_{ij}$ = stoichiometric coefficient of metabolite *i* in reaction *j* - $v_j$ = flux of reaction *j* (mmol·gDW⁻¹·h⁻¹) - $\mu$ = specific growth rate (h⁻¹) **At steady state** $\left(\dfrac{dC_i}{dt}=0\right)$ **and assuming metabolite concentrations are negligible compared to fluxes:** $$ S \cdot v = 0 $$ This is the **cornerstone equation of metabolic flux analysis (MFA) and FBA**. --- ### 2.5 Systems Biology — Top-Down vs Bottom-Up > [!IMPORTANT] > - **Bottom-up Systems Biology:** Starts from *individual components* (genes, proteins), builds mechanistic ODE models, integrates up. E.g., Tyson & Novak cell-cycle model. > - **Top-down Systems Biology:** Starts from *high-throughput omics data*, infers networks statistically (Bayesian, correlation, mutual information), refines downward. E.g., WGCNA co-expression networks. --- ### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (All Key Equations) | # | Equation / Concept | Formula | Variables / Notes | Units | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Mass balance (dynamic)** | $\dfrac{dC_i}{dt} = \sum_{j} S_{ij} v_j - \mu C_i$ | $C_i$: metabolite conc., $S_{ij}$: stoich. coeff., $v_j$: flux, $\mu$: growth rate | mM·h⁻¹ | | 2 | **Steady-state MFA** | $S \cdot v = 0$ | Linear system; $S \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$, $v \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ | dimensionless / mM·h⁻¹ | | 3 | **Degrees of freedom** | $f = n - m - r_{measured}$ | $n$ = reactions, $m$ = metabolites, $r_{measured}$ = measured fluxes; must be $\geq 0$ for solvable system | dimensionless | | 4 | **FBA Objective (e.g., growth)** | $\max Z = c^T v$ | $c$: coefficient vector; maximize biomass yield | dimensionless | | 5 | **FBA constraints** | $S v = 0, \quad v_{\min} \leq v \leq v_{\max}$ | Linear programming problem (LP) | — | | 6 | **Growth rate** | $\mu = c_{biomass}^T v_{biomass}$ | $c_{biomass} \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ — stoichiometry of biomass equation | h⁻¹ | | 7 | **Yield coefficient** | $Y_{X/S} = \dfrac{\Delta X}{-\Delta S}$ | $X$: biomass, $S$: substrate | g·g⁻¹ | | 8 | **Specific uptake rate** | $q_S = \dfrac{1}{X}\dfrac{dS}{dt}$ | Rate per biomass unit | mmol·gDW⁻¹·h⁻¹ | | 9 | **Henderson–Hasselbalch (pH)** | $pH = pK_a + \log\dfrac{[A^-]}{[HA]}$ | Used in buffer design for MS sample prep | dimensionless | | 10 | **Signal-to-noise (RNA-Seq)** | $SNR = \dfrac{\mu_{signal}}{\sigma_{noise}}$ | Determines DEG detection threshold | dimensionless | | 11 | **MS resolution** | $R = \dfrac{m}{\Delta m}$ | $m$: mass, $\Delta m$: peak width at half height | dimensionless | | 12 | **PCA variance explained** | $V_k = \dfrac{\lambda_k}{\sum_i \lambda_i}$ | $\lambda_k$: eigenvalue of PC $k$ | dimensionless | | 13 | **Michaelis–Menten (single enzyme)** | $v = \dfrac{V_{\max}[S]}{K_M + [S]}$ | Used in kinetic modelling of systems biology | mM·h⁻¹ | | 14 | **Enzyme rate law (mass action)** | $v_j = k_j \prod_i [X_i]^{S_{ij}^{sub}}$ | $k_j$: rate constant; substrate powers in stoichiometry | mM·h⁻¹ | > [!NOTE] > **Real-world engineering utility:** > - Equation (2) → **¹³C-MFA** in industrial strain improvement (e.g., lysine, ethanol producers). > - Equation (4)–(5) → **FBA** is used to predict gene knockout targets in *E. coli* and yeast for overproduction of succinate, lycopene, artemisinin precursor, etc. > - Equation (13) → Forms the basis of kinetic models like the **JWS (Jana Wolf Spahn)** online model repository used in academia. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: The Steady-State Mass Balance Equation $S \cdot v = 0$ **Starting Point — General Mass Balance in a CSTR-like biological system:** For metabolite *i* in a well-mixed cellular compartment, the rate of change of its concentration is governed by **all reactions producing it minus all reactions consuming it**, plus dilution by growth. $$ \frac{dC_i}{dt} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} v_j \cdot S_{ij} - \mu \cdot C_i $$ **Step 1 — Explanation of each term:** - $\sum_{j=1}^{n} v_j \cdot S_{ij}$ is the net production of metabolite *i* summed over all *n* reactions. - $S_{ij}$ is the **stoichiometric coefficient**: negative for reactants ($S_{ij} < 0$), positive for products ($S_{ij} > 0$), zero if metabolite *i* is not involved in reaction *j*. - $\mu \cdot C_i$ accounts for **biomass dilution** — when the cell grows, the existing intracellular pool is "diluted" because the cell volume increases. **Step 2 — Apply the quasi-steady-state assumption (QSSA):** The intracellular metabolite pool **turns over in milliseconds to seconds**, much faster than extracellular changes. Therefore, we set $\dfrac{dC_i}{dt} = 0$. $$ 0 = \sum_{j=1}^{n} v_j \cdot S_{ij} - \mu \cdot C_i $$ **Step 3 — Drop the dilution term (standard MFA assumption):** For the **stoichiometric (mass-balanced) MFA**, we further assume $\mu \cdot C_i$ is negligible compared to reaction fluxes (justified because $[\text{intracellular metabolites}] \sim \text{mM}$ while fluxes are integrated over hours). The term is **retained** in **dynamic MFA** but **dropped** in **steady-state MFA / FBA**. $$ 0 = \sum_{j=1}^{n} v_j \cdot S_{ij} $$ **Step 4 — Vectorize (matrix form):** Stacking all *m* metabolites and *n* reactions: $$ S \cdot v = 0 $$ Where: $$ S = \begin{bmatrix} S_{11} & S_{12} & \cdots & S_{1n} \\ S_{21} & S_{22} & \cdots & S_{2n} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ S_{m1} & S_{m2} & \cdots & S_{mn} \end{bmatrix}, \quad v = \begin{bmatrix} v_1 \\ v_2 \\ \vdots \\ v_n \end{bmatrix} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **End result:** A **homogeneous system of linear equations**. The set of all flux vectors *v* satisfying this lives in the **null space (kernel)** of *S*. **FBA seeks a particular *v* in this null space that also maximizes the linear objective function $c^T v$.** --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Degrees of Freedom in a Metabolic Network **Why is this important?** To solve $S \cdot v = 0$ uniquely (or to a small finite set), we must measure some fluxes independently. The number of fluxes we must measure is determined by linear algebra. **Step 1 — Define the dimensions:** - Number of metabolites: $m$ - Number of reactions: $n$ - Number of linearly independent (measured/known) fluxes: $r$ **Step 2 — State the nullity-rank theorem:** For any matrix $S$ of size $m \times n$: $$ \text{rank}(S) + \dim(\text{null}(S)) = n $$ **Step 3 — Maximum rank** of *S* (assuming no redundant rows): The rank cannot exceed $\min(m, n)$. In a typical genome-scale model, $m < n$ (e.g., for *E. coli* iJO1366: $m = 1805$ metabolites, $n = 2583$ reactions, $\text{rank} = m$). **Step 4 — Define degrees of freedom:** The dimension of the null space, minus the number of fluxes we constrain, is the number of **unmeasured, free fluxes**: $$ f = n - m - r $$ **Step 5 — Interpretation:** - $f = 0$ → system is **exactly determined** — solution unique. - $f > 0$ → system is **under-determined** — infinitely many solutions exist; **optimization (FBA)** or **additional constraints** are required. - $f < 0$ → system is **over-determined** — measurement errors must be reconciled via **least-squares MFA**. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** This tells the metabolic engineer *exactly how many* ¹³C-labelling experiments or extracellular flux measurements are needed before the intracellular flux map is computable. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Linear Programming Formulation of FBA **Step 1 — Define decision variable:** $v \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ — vector of unknown reaction fluxes. **Step 2 — Define the linear objective function:** For a typical microbial FBA, the objective is **biomass maximization**: $$ \max_{v} \; Z = c_{biomass}^T v $$ where $c_{biomass}$ is the stoichiometry of the **biomass reaction** (a pseudo-reaction representing all precursors and their molar requirements to form 1 g of biomass). **Step 3 — Define constraints:** *Mass balance (steady state):* $$ S \cdot v = 0 $$ *Reversibility / capacity:* $$ \alpha_j \leq v_j \leq \beta_j $$ where $\alpha_j = -1000$ (or $-\infty$ in pure LP) for irreversible reactions and $\alpha_j = 0$ for irreversible. $\beta_j$ is the maximum enzyme capacity, often set by $V_{\max}$ estimates or experimental data. *Substrate uptake bound (e.g., glucose):* $$ v_{glc\_uptake} \leq 10 \; \text{mmol·gDW}^{-1}\text{·h}^{-1} $$ **Step 4 — Full LP:** $$ \begin{aligned} \max_{v} \quad & c_{biomass}^T v \\ \text{subject to} \quad & S v = 0 \\ & \alpha \leq v \leq \beta \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Solution method:** Solved using the **simplex algorithm** or interior-point methods. Tools: **COBRApy (Python), MATLAB COBRA Toolbox, GAMS**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Limitation of FBA (KTU Pitfall):** FBA gives a *single optimal solution* but the null space is often huge. **Flux Variability Analysis (FVA)** is used to find the **range of feasible fluxes** for each reaction at the optimum. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choice of "Omics" Technique for a Given Engineering Goal | Engineering Goal | Recommended Primary Tool | Secondary Validation | Why | |---|---|---|---| | Find rate-limiting enzyme | **Transcriptomics (RNA-Seq)** | Proteomics (targeted SRM) | High correlation of mRNA & protein for highly expressed genes | | Discover novel biosynthetic gene cluster | **Comparative Genomics (antiSMASH)** | Heterologous expression | Biosynthetic clusters are evolutionarily conserved | | Quantify in vivo pathway flux | **¹³C-MFA / FBA** | NMR + GC-MS | Direct metabolic readout | | Identify PPIs around a target enzyme | **AP-MS (Affinity Purification-MS)** | Y2H, BioID | Lower false-positive rate than Y2H | | Detect PTM relevant to activity | **Phosphoproteomics (TiO₂ enrichment + LC-MS/MS)** | Western blot | Site-specific resolution | | Screen for novel metabolite biomarkers | **Untargeted LC-MS/MS** | Targeted MRM validation | Discovery + verification paradigm | | Build genome-scale kinetic model | **Proteomics (absolute quantification)** | Transcriptomics | Protein concentration → rate constant | | Time-resolved stress response | **Time-series RNA-Seq + metabolomics** | qRT-PCR | Captures dynamics | ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Diagram A — The Omics Cascade in Metabolic Engineering (Concept Map) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Genome
DNA Sequence] --> B[Transcriptome
mRNA via RNA-Seq] B --> C[Proteome
Proteins via LC-MS MS] C --> D[Metabolome
Metabolites via GC-MS NMR] D --> E[Phenotype
Growth Product Titre] F[Functional Genomics] -.->|Interrogates| A F -.->|Interrogates| B G[Proteomics] -.->|Interrogates| C H[Metabolomics] -.->|Interrogates| D I[Systems Biology] -.->|Integrates All| A I -.->|Integrates All| B I -.->|Integrates All| C I -.->|Integrates All| D I -.->|Predicts| E ``` --- ### 4.2 Diagram B — Generic FBA Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph inputA[INPUTS] A1[Annotated Genome FASTA] A2[Biochemical Databases KEGG BRENDA] A3[Biomass Equation] end subgraph reconA[RECONSTRUCTION] B1[Gene Protein Reaction GPR Mapping] B2[Stoichiometric Matrix S] B3[Reaction Bounds alpha beta] end subgraph mathA[MATHEMATICAL FORMULATION] C1[Mass Balance S v = 0] C2[Objective cT v max] C3[Linear Program LP Solver] end subgraph outA[OUTPUTS] D1[Optimal Flux Distribution v opt] D2[Growth Rate mu prediction] D3[Gene Knockout Targets] D4[FVA Range per Reaction] end A1 --> B1 A2 --> B1 A3 --> B1 B1 --> B2 B2 --> B3 B2 --> C1 A3 --> C2 B3 --> C1 C1 --> C3 C2 --> C3 C3 --> D1 D1 --> D2 D1 --> D3 D1 --> D4 ``` --- ### 4.3 Diagram C — Proteomics MS Workflow (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Cell Lysate] --> P2[Protein Extraction] P2 --> P3[Trypsin Digestion] P3 --> P4[Peptide Mixture] P4 --> P5[LC Separation] P5 --> P6[ESI Ionization] P6 --> P7[Quadrupole MS1] P7 --> P8[CID Fragmentation] P8 --> P9[MS2 Spectrum] P9 --> P10[Database Search SEQUEST MASCOT] P10 --> P11[Protein Identifications] ``` --- ### 4.4 Diagram D — Decision Tree for Choosing Omics Strategy ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1{What is the engineering question?} Q1 -->|Which gene is on| R1[Transcriptomics RNA-Seq] Q1 -->|What is the protein doing| R2[Proteomics LC-MS] Q1 -->|What small molecules are made| R3[Metabolomics GC-MS LC-MS NMR] Q1 -->|How does the system behave as a whole| R4[Systems Biology FBA ODE Model] R1 --> S1[Validate by qRT PCR] R2 --> S2[Validate by Western Blot] R3 --> S3[Validate by authentic standards] R4 --> S4[Validate by gene knockouts] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Safeguards Used:** All node IDs are alphanumeric-prefixed (e.g., `A1`, `B1`, `P1`, `Q1`); node labels contain only raw uppercase alphanumeric text with no markdown formatting inside double-quoted labels. ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Set B]** **Define the metabolome and distinguish between targeted and untargeted metabolomics with one example technique for each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - *Definition (1 mark):* The metabolome is the complete set of small-molecule metabolites (typically <1500 Da) present in a biological system at a given physiological state. - *Targeted (1 mark):* Quantifies a predefined set of metabolites with high sensitivity and accuracy. Example: **MRM-based triple-quadrupole LC-MS/MS** for amino acids. - *Untargeted (1 mark):* Unbiased, global survey of all ionizable metabolites, followed by statistical deconvolution. Example: **GC-MS-based metabolic fingerprinting** of *E. coli* fermentation broth. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **What is the stoichiometric matrix in a metabolic model? What is its typical size for the *E. coli* genome-scale model iJO1366?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - *Definition (2 marks):* The stoichiometric matrix **S** is an $m \times n$ matrix that lists the stoichiometric coefficients of all *m* metabolites in all *n* reactions. $S_{ij}$ is negative for substrates, positive for products, and zero if metabolite *i* is not involved in reaction *j*. It enables the steady-state mass balance $S \cdot v = 0$. - *Size (1 mark):* For *E. coli* iJO1366, $m = 1805$ metabolites, $n = 2583$ reactions (approx.). --- ### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) --- #### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **(a)** Explain the workflow of **mass spectrometry-based proteomics**, distinguishing **ESI** and **MALDI** ionization techniques. Briefly describe the role of database search engines. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A simplified bacterial pathway converts glucose (GLC) to pyruvate (PYR) via the following stoichiometry: - R1: GLC + ATP → G6P + ADP - R2: G6P → F6P - R3: F6P + ATP → 2 PYR + 2 ADP Write the **stoichiometric matrix S** and apply the **steady-state assumption** to derive the flux balance equation $S v = 0$. If ATP and ADP are lumped as a single pseudo-cofactor pair with $v_3 = 2$ mmol·gDW⁻¹·h⁻¹, compute all other fluxes. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Answer (a) — 7 marks:** 1. **Sample preparation (1 mark):** Cell lysis, protein extraction, reduction/alkylation, tryptic digestion → peptide mixture. 2. **Separation (1 mark):** Reverse-phase **Liquid Chromatography (LC)** separates peptides by hydrophobicity. 3. **Ionization (2 marks):** - **ESI** — soft ionization, produces multiply charged ions, ideal for on-line LC coupling and intact proteins. - **MALDI** — uses a UV-absorbing matrix, produces mostly singly charged ions, ideal for high-throughput peptide mass fingerprinting. 4. **Mass analysis + MS/MS (1 mark):** Quadrupole/Orbitrap selects precursor ions; CID fragments them into b/y ions; MS2 spectrum recorded. 5. **Database search (1 mark):** Software like **SEQUEST, MASCOT, Andromeda** match MS2 spectra to *in-silico* digests of protein databases to identify proteins. 6. **Quantification (1 mark):** Label-free (intensity) or isotopic (SILAC, TMT, iTRAQ) approaches yield relative/absolute abundance. --- **Model Answer (b) — 7 marks:** **Step 1 — Identify metabolites and reactions (1 mark):** Metabolites: {GLC, ATP, G6P, ADP, F6P, PYR} → $m = 6$. Reactions: R1, R2, R3 → $n = 3$. **Step 2 — Construct S (2 marks):** $$ S = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 & 0 & \text{GLC} \\ -1 & 0 & -1 & \text{ATP} \\ +1 & -1 & 0 & \text{G6P} \\ +1 & 0 & +1 & \text{ADP} \\ 0 & +1 & -1 & \text{F6P} \\ 0 & 0 & +2 & \text{PYR} \end{bmatrix} $$ (Columns = R1, R2, R3; Rows in the order GLC, ATP, G6P, ADP, F6P, PYR.) **Step 3 — Write steady-state mass balance (1 mark):** $$ S \cdot v = 0 \quad \text{where} \quad v = \begin{bmatrix} v_1 \\ v_2 \\ v_3 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 4 — Compute (3 marks):** Given $v_3 = 2$ mmol·gDW⁻¹·h⁻¹. From **PYR** row: $0\cdot v_1 + 0\cdot v_2 + 2 v_3 = 0$ → wait, PYR is *produced* (positive coefficient $+2$): $$ +2 v_3 = 0 \;\text{? NO, this is net balance; PYR has no other consumption listed, so v3 must equal the export flux.} $$ Re-formulating: the network produces PYR that is exported via an additional reaction R4: PYR → ∅ with rate $v_4$. Then the full system becomes $S'v' = 0$ with $v' = [v_1, v_2, v_3, v_4]^T$ and PYR row giving $2 v_3 - v_4 = 0 \Rightarrow v_4 = 4$ mmol·gDW⁻¹·h⁻¹. From **F6P** row: $v_2 - v_3 = 0 \Rightarrow v_2 = v_3 = 2$ mmol·gDW⁻¹·h⁻¹. From **G6P** row: $v_1 - v_2 = 0 \Rightarrow v_1 = v_2 = 2$ mmol·gDW⁻¹·h⁻¹. From **ATP/ADP** row: $-v_1 + v_3 = 0 \Rightarrow v_1 = v_3$ ✓ (consistent). **Final answer:** $v_1 = 2, \; v_2 = 2, \; v_3 = 2, \; v_4 = 4$ mmol·gDW⁻¹·h⁻¹. --- #### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **(a)** Define **systems biology** and explain the difference between **bottom-up** and **top-down** approaches with suitable examples. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Define **Flux Balance Analysis (FBA)**. For a network with stoichiometric matrix $S \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ and a biomass objective $c \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$, write the complete LP formulation. A simplified model has the following LP: $$ \max \; v_{\text{bio}}, \quad S v = 0, \quad 0 \leq v \leq 10 $$ Suppose the optimal $v_{\text{bio}} = 1.8$ h⁻¹. Comment on the biological meaning of the upper bound 10. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Answer (a) — 7 marks:** 1. **Definition (2 marks):** Systems biology is the holistic, integrative study of biological systems in which all components (genes, transcripts, proteins, metabolites) and their interactions are simultaneously analysed as a mathematically modelled, dynamic network, with the goal of understanding emergent properties. 2. **Bottom-up (2.5 marks):** - Starts from *individual molecular components* (single gene, single enzyme). - Builds **mechanistic ODE/PDE models** from kinetic parameters ($k_{cat}$, $K_M$). - Integrates upward to predict system-level behaviour. - **Example:** Tyson & Novak (2001) cell-cycle model of the budding yeast. 3. **Top-down (2.5 marks):** - Starts from *high-throughput omics data* (transcriptomics, proteomics). - Infers **statistical / reverse-engineered networks** (Bayesian, mutual information, WGCNA). - Refines downward to candidate components. - **Example:** Microbial co-expression network of *E. coli* under anaerobic stress. --- **Model Answer (b) — 7 marks:** 1. **FBA definition (2 marks):** Flux Balance Analysis is a constraint-based mathematical method that predicts the steady-state flux distribution through a metabolic network by solving a linear programming (LP) problem, using the stoichiometric matrix as the principal constraint. 2. **Full LP formulation (3 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} \max_{v \in \mathbb{R}^n} \quad & c^T v \\ \text{subject to} \quad & S v = 0 \\ & v_{\min} \leq v \leq v_{\max} \end{aligned} $$ where $c$ is the objective coefficient vector (e.g., the biomass reaction with $c_i = 1$ for $i = \text{biomass}$ and $c_i = 0$ otherwise). 3. **Bound interpretation (2 marks):** The upper bound 10 mmol·gDW⁻¹·h⁻¹ represents the **maximum allowable flux** through every reaction — typically reflecting either **enzyme capacity** ($V_{\max} = k_{cat}[E]$), **thermodynamic feasibility**, or **substrate uptake rate** (e.g., glucose transport). In practice, bounds are set per reaction, not globally; the uniform 10 here is illustrative. The fact that $v_{\text{bio}}^* = 1.8$ is well below 10 indicates that growth is **not constrained by enzyme capacity** but by **network stoichiometry** (e.g., limited precursor supply). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Equation (2) requires the steady-state assumption explicitly stated** — losing the phrase "quasi-steady-state" or "dC/dt = 0" costs 1 mark. > 2. **FBA ≠ MFA**: FBA is *predictive optimization*; MFA is *measurement-driven* flux estimation. Students frequently confuse them. > 3. **In Q1 (Part B-b) above:** PYR is a *product*, not consumed internally — students forget to add an export reaction and incorrectly conclude the system has no solution. > 4. **Stoichiometric matrix sign convention:** S is **negative for substrates**, **positive for products**. Reversing the sign is a 1-mark deduction. > 5. **Y2H gives PPI *candidates*, not validated interactions** — always include a validation step (Co-IP) in your answer. --- ### **TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER** (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [ ] The **four "omes"** (genome, transcriptome, proteome, metabolome) form a **cascade**, and the four "omics" disciplines (functional genomics, proteomics, metabolomics, systems biology) interrogate **successive links** in this cascade. - [ ] **Functional genomics** = gene function on a genome scale; key tool = **RNA-Seq**. - [ ] **Proteomics** = proteins + PTMs; key tools = **LC-MS/MS, Y2H, AP-MS**; ESI vs MALDI is a perennial KTU question. - [ ] **Metabolomics** = small-molecule snapshot of phenotype; **targeted** (MRM) vs **untargeted** (full-scan MS or NMR). - [ ] **Systems biology** integrates all omics with **predictive models**; **bottom-up (mechanistic)** vs **top-down (statistical)**. - [ ] **Steady-state mass balance:** $S \cdot v = 0$ — the central equation of constraint-based modelling. - [ ] **Degrees of freedom:** $f = n - m - r$ — must be $\geq 0$ for an exactly or under-determined system. - [ ] **FBA LP formulation:** $\max c^T v$ s.t. $S v = 0$, $v_{\min} \leq v \leq v_{\max}$. - [ ] **FBA does not use kinetics** — purely stoichiometric; for kinetics, use **ODE-based dynamic models**. - [ ] **FVA (Flux Variability Analysis)** is the post-FBA tool to find the **feasible range** of each flux at optimality. - [ ] **¹³C-MFA** uses isotopic labelling + NMR/MS to **measure** fluxes; FBA uses constraints to **predict** them. - [ ] **Sij sign convention:** negative for substrates, positive for products — write it explicitly in exams. - [ ] **iJO1366** (*E. coli*) has ≈ **1805 metabolites, 2583 reactions**; a frequently cited benchmark model. - [ ] **Y2H false-positive rate ≈ 30–50%** — always pair with **Co-IP or BioID** validation. - [ ] **MALDI** = singly charged ions, pulsed; **ESI** = multiply charged ions, continuous, on-line LC compatible. - [ ] **Henderson–Hasselbalch** equation is occasionally used in MS sample-prep buffer design. - [ ] **Central dogma extension to function:** Genome → Transcriptome → Proteome → Metabolome → Phenotype. - [ ] **Top-down systems biology** uses tools like **WGCNA, ARACNE, Bayesian networks**; bottom-up uses **Tyson-Novak, Boolean models, ODEs**. - [ ] **PCA variance ratio** $V_k = \lambda_k / \sum \lambda_i$ is the standard for choosing how many principal components to retain in metabolomics. - [ ] **Growth rate** $\mu$ in FBA = $c_{biomass}^T v_{biomass}$ (biomass reaction flux). - [ ] **Yield coefficient** $Y_{X/S} = \Delta X / (-\Delta S)$ — a classical metabolic engineering metric.

topics.title: Application of Metabolic Engineering: Enhancement of Product Yield, Alteration of Nitrogen Metabolism, Production of Antibiotics, Vitamins, Polyketides, etc., Bioconversions, Case Studies from Research Articles
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Application of Metabolic Engineering: Product Yield, Nitrogen Metabolism & Industrial Bioproducts

## 1.1 Core Definition & Scope

**Metabolic Engineering (ME)** is the targeted modification of cellular biochemical networks — through genetic, regulatory, or enzymatic manipulation — to enhance the production of desired metabolites, expand substrate utilization, or create entirely novel biological functions. The **applications module** moves beyond *in silico* flux analysis (covered in Module 3) into *in vivo* and *industrial* implementation of pathway rewiring.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (NEP-Aligned):** *"Application of Metabolic Engineering is the rational redirection of carbon, nitrogen, and energy flux through the use of recombinant DNA technology, omics-data integration, and systems biology tools, to convert a microorganism into an efficient cellular factory for industrial products, pharmaceuticals, and specialty chemicals."*

The application spectrum spans **four industrial pillars**:

| Industrial Pillar | Example Target Molecule | Typical Titer (Industrial) |
|---|---|---|
| Primary Metabolites | L-Glutamate, L-Lysine | > 100 g/L |
| Secondary Metabolites | Penicillin G, Erythromycin | 30 – 70 g/L |
| Vitamins | Riboflavin (B₂), B₁₂ | 10 – 50 g/L |
| Polyketides | Erythromycin, Rapamycin | 1 – 10 g/L |

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Cellular Factory" Analogy

Imagine a **car-assembly factory** (the microbial cell) with hundreds of conveyor belts (metabolic pathways). Originally, the factory was built to keep itself running (growth, maintenance). A metabolic engineer is a **plant manager** who:

1. **Adds new machines** (heterologous gene insertion) to assemble a new product.
2. **Speeds up specific belts** (overexpression of rate-limiting enzymes).
3. **Stops competing lines** (knockout of by-product pathways).
4. **Re-routes raw material delivery** (precursor redirection).
5. **Adds a dedicated quality-control engineer** (efflux transporters to remove toxic intermediates).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students:** Every successful metabolic engineering case study essentially addresses the same central question: *"How do we push more carbon atoms (and nitrogen, where relevant) into the desired product pathway while keeping the cell alive?"*

---

## 1.3 Historical Context & Evolution of the Field

The discipline traces its modern origin to **Jay Bailey (1991)**, who coined "Metabolic Engineering." Three technological waves drove its applications:

- **Wave 1 (1990s):** Single-gene knockouts and amplifications — e.g., *E. coli* lysine overproduction.
- **Wave 2 (2000s):** Systems metabolic engineering — combining omics with genome-scale models (Stephanopoulos group).
- **Wave 3 (2010s–present):** Synthetic biology + CRISPR tools, allowing whole-pathway *de novo* design and dynamic regulation.

---

## 1.4 Core Strategy Framework for Product Yield Enhancement

The product yield $Y_{P/S}$ (grams of product per gram of substrate) is mathematically the **central optimization target**:

$$
Y_{P/S} = \frac{\text{mass of product formed}}{\text{mass of substrate consumed}}
$$

Enhancement strategies operate on five logical levels:

1. **Precursor-level engineering** — Expand the supply of starting metabolites.
2. **Pathway-level engineering** — Remove bottlenecks, eliminate competing branches.
3. **Regulatory-level engineering** — Attenuate feedback inhibition, derepress operons.
4. **Transport-level engineering** — Use efflux systems to reduce intracellular toxicity.
5. **Genome-level engineering** — Whole-genome mutagenesis, adaptive laboratory evolution (ALE).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Theoretical Basis of Yield Enhancement

Product overproduction fundamentally violates a wild-type cell's natural design principle: **homeostasis**. Wild-type microbes have evolved **catabolite repression, feedback inhibition, and growth-coupled regulation** to *prevent* excess metabolite accumulation. Therefore, yield enhancement = **disarming these natural control mechanisms** + **installing synthetic drive**.

### 2.1.1 Quantitative Targets

| Metric | Symbol | Definition | Typical Industrial Goal |
|---|---|---|---|
| Yield on substrate | $Y_{P/S}$ | g product / g substrate | Approach the theoretical maximum |
| Productivity | $Q_P$ | g product / (L · h) | Maximize volumetric throughput |
| Titer | $[P]$ | g product / L | High enough for cost-effective DSP |
| Specific productivity | $q_P$ | g product / (g DCW · h) | Measure of cellular efficiency |

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet & Key Engineering Equations

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Units / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\displaystyle Y_{P/S}^{\max} = \frac{\nu_P}{\nu_S}$ | Max yield = stoichiometric ratio of product to substrate consumption | g/g (dimensionless) |
| 2 | $\displaystyle \Delta G_R = \Delta G_R^{'o} + RT \ln Q$ | Thermodynamic feasibility of pathway; must be $<0$ for spontaneity | kJ/mol |
| 3 | $\displaystyle \mu = \frac{1}{X}\frac{dX}{dt}$ | Specific growth rate | h⁻¹ |
| 4 | $\displaystyle q_P = \frac{1}{X}\frac{dP}{dt}$ | Specific product formation rate | g/(g·h) |
| 5 | $\displaystyle \sigma = \frac{Y_{P/S}^{\text{actual}}}{Y_{P/S}^{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100\%$ | Carbon efficiency / yield coefficient | % (0–100) |
| 6 | $\displaystyle \text{C-mol yield} = \frac{\text{C-mol product}}{\text{C-mol substrate}}$ | Atom-balanced carbon yield | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\displaystyle v = V_{\max}\frac{[S]}{K_M + [S]}$ | Michaelis–Menten kinetics for rate-limiting step | varies |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Pitfall:** The vertical pipe symbol `|` inside markdown tables breaks formatting. In all tables of these notes, absolute value is written as `\vert` or described textually. When students copy-paste formulas, they should never manually place `|` between numbers inside a table row.

---

## 2.3 Theoretical Analysis of Each Application Domain

### 2.3.1 Enhancement of Product Yield (Generic Framework)

For any target metabolite, the engineering logic follows a **5-tier cascade**:

1. **Identify the rate-limiting step** — Flux Balance Analysis (FBA), transcriptomics.
2. **Eliminate competing branches** — Gene knockouts (Δ*ldhA*, Δ*pta*, Δ*adhE* in *E. coli*).
3. **Amplify the bottleneck enzyme** — Plasmid overexpression or chromosomal promoter replacement.
4. **Block feedback inhibition** — Express feedback-resistant mutants (e.g., *lysC*^T311I^ in lysine pathway).
5. **Improve precursor availability** — Redirect TCA carbon toward oxaloacetate or acetyl-CoA pools.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** This framework is the working template in every industrial strain improvement program at companies like Novozymes, DSM, and Ajinomoto.

### 2.3.2 Alteration of Nitrogen Metabolism

Nitrogen metabolism engineering has **three industrial foci**:

- **Amino acid overproduction** (glutamate, lysine, threonine) — requires manipulating the **ammonium assimilation** pathway (glutamine synthetase / glutamate synthase GS-GOGAT cycle).
- **Nitrogen fixation enhancement** — Engineering *nif* regulon in *Klebsiella* or *Azotobacter* to enable non-legume-associated nitrogen fixation.
- **C/N balance optimization** — High-yield industrial fermentations require careful C:N ratios (typically 5:1 to 20:1) to avoid Crabtree-like overflow metabolism.

#### Key Equation — GS-GOGAT Cycle Stoichiometry

$$
\text{NH}_4^+ + \alpha\text{-KG} + \text{ATP} \xrightarrow{\text{GS}} \text{L-Gln} + \text{ADP} + P_i
$$

$$
\text{L-Gln} + \alpha\text{-KG} + \text{NADPH} \xrightarrow{\text{GOGAT}} 2 \text{ L-Glu} + \text{NADP}^+
$$

> Net: 1 NH₄⁺ + 2 α-KG + 1 ATP + 1 NADPH → 2 L-Glu + 1 ADP + P₁ + 1 NADP⁺

Industrial lysine producers (e.g., *Corynebacterium glutamicum*) engineer this cycle by **removing feedback inhibition of aspartokinase** and **amplifying dihydrodipicolinate reductase**.

### 2.3.3 Production of Antibiotics

Antibiotics are predominantly **secondary metabolites**, produced by *Streptomyces*, *Penicillium*, *Acremonium*. The three major engineered classes are:

| Class | Example | Native Host | Engineering Target |
|---|---|---|---|
| β-Lactams | Penicillin G | *P. chrysogenum* | Increase precursor PAA flux |
| Macrolides | Erythromycin | *Saccharopolyspora erythraea* | Amplify DEBS (6-deoxyerythronolide B synthase) |
| Aminoglycosides | Streptomycin | *Streptomyces griseus* | Derepress *str* gene cluster |
| Glycopeptides | Vancomycin | *Amycolatopsis orientalis* | Activate silent BGCs |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Logic for β-Lactams:** The Penicillin precursor **phenylacetic acid (PAA)** is the central chokepoint. Overexpressing the **phenylacetyl-CoA ligase (PCL)** and **isopenicillin N acyltransferase (IAT)** pushes 60–80% of the PAA pool toward penicillin G.

### 2.3.4 Production of Vitamins

Industrial vitamin bioprocesses are dominated by **two microbial workhorses**:

- ***Ashbya gossypii*** — Riboflavin (B₂) production, titer > 20 g/L.
- ***Pseudomonas denitrificans*** / *Sinorhizobium meliloti* — Vitamin B₁₂ (cobalamin).

#### Riboflavin Pathway Logic

The riboflavin operon (*RIB*) in *B. subtilis* encodes 5 enzymes. The engineering cascade:

1. Overexpress **GTP cyclohydrolase II** (rate-limiting).
2. Knock out **riboflavin kinase** (downstream salvage) to *trap* riboflavin.
3. Disrupt **repressor *ribC*** (FAD-dependent).
4. Mutate *pur* regulon to boost GTP precursor.

### 2.3.5 Production of Polyketides

Polyketides are synthesized by **Polyketide Synthases (PKS)** — giant multi-modular megasynthases (Type I) or dissociated complexes (Type II). The molecular logic:

- Each **module** adds one acyl-CoA extender unit (malonyl-CoA, methylmalonyl-CoA).
- The **chain length, reduction pattern, and starter unit** determine the chemical scaffold.

#### 6-Deoxyerythronolide B Synthase (DEBS)

The DEBS in *S. erythraea* is a 6-module Type I PKS producing the macrolactone 6-dEB — the erythromycin aglycone. Engineering opportunities:

- **Module swapping** to alter chain length.
- **Domain substitution** (KS-AT-ACP or KR-DH-ER) to change reduction state.
- **Starter unit engineering** via loading didomain swap.
- **Heterologous expression** in *E. coli* or *S. cerevisiae* using the Keating–Khosla platform.

### 2.3.6 Bioconversions

Bioconversions (also called **biotransformations**) use whole cells or isolated enzymes to convert a substrate into a higher-value product, often involving **a single chemical step** or a **few steps** that are difficult by chemical synthesis.

Classic examples:
- **Steroid bioconversion** — Rhizopus arrhizus converts progesterone → 11α-hydroxyprogesterone (used in oral contraceptive synthesis).
- **L-Sorbose production** — *Gluconobacter oxydans* converts D-sorbitol → L-sorbose (Vitamin C precursor).
- **Acyloin condensation** — *Saccharomyces* and *Lactobacillus* convert benzaldehyde → L-phenylacetylcarbinol (L-PAC, ephedrine precursor).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bioconversion vs Fermentation:** In fermentation, the product is synthesized *de novo* from a simple carbon source (sugar). In bioconversion, the product results from a *specific chemical modification* of an externally supplied substrate. The latter is essentially **enzymatic chemistry on a biological platform.**

---

## 2.4 Cross-Cutting Engineering Strategies

### 2.4.1 Global Regulatory Mutagenesis
Mutations in global regulators (*relA*, *spoT*, *crp*, *fnr*, *pho*) globally alter carbon/nitrogen flux.

### 2.4.2 Transporter Engineering
Efflux transporters reduce intracellular product toxicity:
- **LysE** in *C. glutamicum* for lysine export.
- **Mmr** and **MFS-type** exporters in antibiotic producers.

### 2.4.3 Cofactor Engineering
Balancing NADH/NADPH is critical for redox-intensive products (e.g., lysine = 4 NADPH per mole; riboflavin = significant FAD).

### 2.4.4 Dynamic Regulation
Toggle switches, riboswitches, and quorum-sensing circuits allow pathway activation only after growth phase.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

These strategies translate into industrial impact in:
- **Pharma:** Antibiotic supply at multi-ton scale.
- **Food/Feed:** Amino acids (3 million tonnes/yr globally for L-lysine alone).
- **Agriculture:** Microbial nitrogen-fixation inoculants (reducing synthetic N-fertilizer use).
- **Cosmetics/Specialty:** Polyketide-derived pigments, fragrances, and immunosuppressants (rapamycin, FK506).
- **Chemical Industry:** Vitamin C (Reichstein process replaced by a hybrid bio-chemical route using *Gluconobacter*).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Theoretical Maximum Yield — Worked Example (Lysine)

This derivation mirrors what the KTU examiner expects in **Part B numerical/analytical problems.**

### Problem Setup

*Biosynthesis of L-Lysine from glucose in* Corynebacterium glutamicum. *The overall pathway involves 8 enzymatic steps, branching from the TCA intermediate oxaloacetate.*

We want to calculate **$Y_{P/S}^{\max}$** in C-mol basis.

### Step 1 — Write the Overall Pathway Stoichiometry

$$
1 \text{ Glucose} + 2 \text{ NH}_4^+ + 4 \text{ NADPH} + 4 \text{ ATP} \rightarrow 1 \text{ L-Lysine} + 4 \text{ NADP}^+ + 4 \text{ ADP} + 4 P_i
$$

### Step 2 — Identify the Carbon-Balance

Glucose = **6 carbons** (6 C-mol).
L-Lysine = **6 carbons** (6 C-mol).

Therefore, on a **C-mol basis**, the theoretical maximum carbon yield is:

$$
Y_{P/S}^{\max} = \frac{6 \text{ C-mol Lysine}}{6 \text{ C-mol Glucose}} = 1.00 \; (100\%)
$$

### Step 3 — Convert to Mass Yield

Molecular weights: Glucose = 180 g/mol; L-Lysine (free base) = 146.2 g/mol.

$$
Y_{P/S}^{\text{mass}} = \frac{1 \text{ mol Lys} \times 146.2 \text{ g/mol}}{1 \text{ mol Glc} \times 180 \text{ g/mol}} = 0.812 \; \text{g Lys / g Glc}
$$

### Step 4 — Account for Biomass and CO₂ Loss

Wild-type *C. glutamicum* uses ~50% of glucose carbon for biomass. Industrial strains have been engineered (Δ*sucCD*, Δ*ldh*, feedback-resistant *lysC*) to redirect ~75% toward lysine.

$$
Y_{P/S}^{\text{industrial, actual}} \approx 0.55 \text{ g Lys / g Glc}
$$

$$
\sigma = \frac{0.55}{0.81} \times 100\% \approx 68\%
$$

### Step 5 — Interpretation

Even world-class industrial strains achieve only **~68% of theoretical maximum** because:
- Maintenance energy (ATP turnover).
- By-product formation (trehalose, glutamate).
- Overflow metabolism (glycerol excretion).
- Imperfect redox balance.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Penicillin G Theoretical Yield from Glucose

### Step 1 — Define the Stoichiometric Equation

The simplified Pen G pathway:

$$
1 \text{ PAA (Phe-derived)} + 1 \text{ L-Cys} + 1 \text{ L-Val} + 3 \text{ NH}_4^+ + 8 \text{ ATP} \rightarrow 1 \text{ Pen G} + \text{by-products}
$$

### Step 2 — Recognize Branching

Phenylacetic acid (PAA) comes from chorismate (shikimate pathway). L-Cys and L-Val come from aspartate and pyruvate nodes, respectively.

### Step 3 — Glucose Consumption

Approximately **3.5 mol glucose → 1 mol Pen G** in stoichiometric terms.

### Step 4 — Yield

$$
Y_{P/S}^{\max} = \frac{M_{\text{Pen G}}}{3.5 \times M_{\text{Glc}}} = \frac{334.4 \text{ g/mol}}{3.5 \times 180 \text{ g/mol}} \approx 0.531 \; \text{g Pen G / g Glc}
$$

Industrial titers of ~50 g/L with ~150 g/L glucose consumed yield ~0.33 g/g → ~62% of theoretical.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Engineering Targets by Product Class

| Product | Host | Pathway Localization | Bottleneck Enzyme | Key Engineering Intervention |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| L-Glutamate | *C. glutamicum* | TCA branch | Glutamate dehydrogenase | Biotin limitation (membrane leak) |
| L-Lysine | *C. glutamicum* | Aspartate family | Aspartokinase (*lysC*) | T311I feedback-resistant mutant |
| L-Threonine | *E. coli* | Aspartate family | Aspartokinase I / III | Δ*metL*, Δ*lysC*, Δ*thrL* attenuation |
| Penicillin G | *P. chrysogenum* | Non-ribosomal peptide | ACV synthetase (ACVS) | Overexpress *pcbAB*, *penDE* |
| Erythromycin | *S. erythraea* | Type I PKS | DEBS module 1 | Amplify *eryAIII* |
| Streptomycin | *S. griseus* | Aminocyclitol | Amidotransferase | Derepress *strA*, *strB* |
| Riboflavin (B₂) | *A. gossypii* | Purine / GTP branch | GTP cyclohydrolase II | Disrupt *ribR* |
| Vitamin B₁₂ | *P. denitrificans* | Uroporphyrinogen III | Methyltransferases | Cobalamin operon amplification |
| L-Sorbose | *G. oxydans* | Single-step oxidation | PQQ-dependent dehydrogenase | Permeabilized cells, high aeration |
| 11α-Hydroxyprogesterone | *R. arrhizus* | Steroid hydroxylase | Cytochrome P450 | Add 2-oxoglutarate cofactor recycling |

---

## 3.4 Detailed Worked Case — Erythromycin Polyketide Production

### 3.4.1 Modular Architecture of DEBS

DEBS is a homodimeric, 6-module megasynthase:

$$
\underbrace{\text{M1}}_{KS-AT-ACP} \rightarrow \underbrace{\text{M2}}_{KS-AT-DH-ER-KR-ACP} \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow \underbrace{\text{M6}}_{KS-AT-ACP-TE}
$$

### 3.4.2 Carbon Chain Logic

Each module adds one **methylmalonyl-CoA (MMCoA)** extender (3 carbons). Six additions:

$$
\text{Starter (1C)} + 6 \times \text{MMCoA (3C each)} = 19 \text{ C-atoms in 6-dEB}
$$

### 3.4.3 Yield Limitation in Native *S. erythraea*

- MMCoA pool is supplied from propionate via methylmalonyl-CoA mutase or from succinyl-CoA → MMCoA epimerase.
- MMCoA scarcity limits flux.

### 3.4.4 Engineering Solution

Overexpress **propionyl-CoA carboxylase complex (*pccB*)** from *S. coelicolor* to channel propionate → MMCoA. This boost has raised erythromycin titers by 3–5 fold in engineered strains.

---

## 3.5 Detailed Worked Case — Nitrogen Metabolism Engineering in *C. glutamicum*

### Objective: Boost L-Lysine via NH₄⁺ flux redirection

**Step A — Baseline GS-GOGAT flux.**
GS-GOGAT cycle converts NH₄⁺ → L-Gln → L-Glu. L-Glu → L-Asp → L-Lys (via aspartate kinase branch).

**Step B — Engineering changes:**

1. **Mutate *lysC* (Aspartokinase):** T311I → feedback-resistant → no L-Thr inhibition.
2. **Attenuate *thrA*:** Reduce threonine branch by ~70%.
3. **Overexpress *dapA* (DHPS):** Push flux toward lysine.
4. **Amplify *lysE* exporter:** Reduce intracellular feedback accumulation.

**Step C — Outcome.**
Lysine titer: < 1 g/L (wild-type) → 100+ g/L (industrial strains).

---

## 3.6 Mathematical Model — Dynamic Growth–Production Trade-off

$$
\mu_{\text{apparent}} = \mu_{\max} \left(1 - \frac{P}{P_{\max}}\right)
$$

This is a simplified **logistic-type** model describing how intracellular product accumulation reduces growth rate.

For product formation:

$$
\frac{dP}{dt} = q_P \cdot X = \left( q_{P,\max} \cdot \frac{S}{K_S + S} \right) \cdot X
$$

Students should know this **coupled differential form** for KTU numerical problems on fed-batch fermentations.

---

## 3.7 Thermodynamic Feasibility Check — Example Calculation

### Pathway: Glucose → Ethanol (2 NADH consumed)

$$
\Delta G_R^{'o} = -235 \text{ kJ/mol (glycolysis)} - 2 \times ( -41 \text{ kJ/mol (NADH reoxidation)})
$$

### Step-by-step

$$
\Delta G_R^{'o}(\text{ethanol from glucose}) \approx -235 + 82 = -153 \text{ kJ/mol}
$$

A negative value confirms **thermodynamic spontaneity**. For artificial pathways (e.g., engineered glycerol → 1,3-propanediol), one must verify every node.

---

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Generic Metabolic Engineering Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    goalA[Define Target Product] --> goalB[Identify Native Pathway]
    goalB --> goalC[Perform Flux Balance Analysis]
    goalC --> goalD[Identify Bottleneck Nodes]
    goalD --> goalE[Knockout Competing Branches]
    goalD --> goalF[Overexpress Rate Limiting Enzyme]
    goalE --> goalG[Integrate Regulatory Modifications]
    goalF --> goalG
    goalG --> goalH[Implement Transporter Engineering]
    goalH --> goalI[Validate In Vivo Flux]
    goalI --> goalJ{Industrial Targets Met?}
    goalJ -- No --> goalC
    goalJ -- Yes --> goalK[Scale Up Bioprocess]
```

```mermaid
    aspsA --> dapA[Dihydrodipicolinate]
    dapA --> lysA[L-Lysine]

    aspkA -.Feedback inhibition.-> thrA[Threonine branch]
    thrA --> thrX[Threonine]

```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
```

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]
**Define metabolic engineering and list any four major application areas.**

**Model Answer:**
Metabolic engineering is the purposeful modification of biochemical pathways using recombinant DNA technology, omics data, and systems biology to redirect cellular flux toward a desired product. The four major application areas are:
1. Enhancement of primary metabolite yield (e.g., amino acids).
2. Production of secondary metabolites (e.g., antibiotics).
3. Bioconversions and whole-cell biocatalysis.
4. Vitamin and polyketide biosynthesis.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Set B]
**What is the GS-GOGAT cycle and why is it significant in nitrogen metabolism engineering?**

**Model Answer:**
The GS-GOGAT cycle is the two-step ammonium assimilation pathway in which glutamine synthetase (GS) combines NH₄⁺ with glutamate to form glutamine (consuming ATP), and glutamate synthase (GOGAT) transfers the amide nitrogen to α-ketoglutarate to yield two glutamates. It is significant because it serves as the **central entry point for inorganic nitrogen** into all nitrogen-containing biomolecules (amino acids, nucleotides), making it the prime engineering target for amino-acid overproduction and nitrogen-fixing strain development.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question (Internal Choice — 14 Marks)

### Question A — Comprehensive Option (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]**

#### (a) Discuss in detail the various strategies used in metabolic engineering for the enhancement of product yield, with examples. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Introduction (1 mark):** Product yield enhancement involves maximizing $Y_{P/S}$, the grams of product per gram of substrate. Theoretical maximum is determined by stoichiometry and thermodynamics.

2. **Strategy 1 — Precursor Engineering (1 mark):** Boosting the supply of starting metabolites. *Example:* Overexpression of *pckA* (PEP carboxykinase) to raise oxaloacetate pool for lysine.

3. **Strategy 2 — Bottleneck Removal (1 mark):** Identifying and amplifying the rate-limiting enzyme using FBA. *Example:* GTP cyclohydrolase II amplification in riboflavin pathway.

4. **Strategy 3 — Competing Pathway Knockout (1 mark):** Deletion of by-product branches. *Example:* Δ*ldhA*, Δ*pta*, Δ*adhE* in *E. coli* for ethanol.

5. **Strategy 4 — Feedback Deregulation (1 mark):** Expressing feedback-resistant mutants. *Example:* *lysC*^T311I^ in *C. glutamicum* for lysine.

6. **Strategy 5 — Transporter Engineering (1 mark):** Efflux systems to reduce intracellular product toxicity and relieve feedback. *Example:* LysE for lysine export.

7. **Strategy 6 — Global Regulatory Engineering (1 mark):** Mutations in *crp*, *relA*, *spoT* globally alter C/N distribution. *Example:* *relA* mutations enhancing ppGpp pool and secondary metabolite production.

#### (b) Explain the production of polyketides through metabolic engineering, with erythromycin as the case study. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Polyketide Background (1 mark):** Polyketides are secondary metabolites synthesized by PKS enzymes using acyl-CoA building blocks. They include macrolide antibiotics (erythromycin), immunosuppressants (rapamycin), and anticancer agents (epothilone).

2. **DEBS Architecture (2 marks):** The 6-deoxyerythronolide B synthase (DEBS) is a homodimeric, modular Type I PKS. Each module (KS-AT-ACP plus optional KR/DH/ER) adds one methylmalonyl-CoA extender (3 carbons). Six modules generate the 19-carbon 6-dEB macrolactone. The chain is then released by the thioesterase domain, hydroxylated (EryF), and glycosylated (EryBV, EryCIII).

3. **Engineering Strategy 1 — Precursor Supply (1 mark):** Overexpression of *pccB* (propionyl-CoA carboxylase) raises MMCoA pool, increasing erythromycin titers 3–5 fold.

4. **Engineering Strategy 2 — Heterologous Expression (1 mark):** Keating–Khosla platform expresses DEBS in *E. coli* or *S. cerevisiae* with phosphopantetheinyl transferase (PPTase) co-expression for ACP activation.

5. **Engineering Strategy 3 — Module and Domain Engineering (1 mark):** Module substitution, KS/AT swapping, and KR/DH/ER inactivation can produce erythromycin analogs (e.g., 6-dEB analogs with altered reduction states) — the foundation of "combinatorial biosynthesis."

6. **Conclusion (1 mark):** Polyketide engineering enables both yield improvement and the discovery of novel analogs through rational reprogramming of PKS modules.

---

### Question B — Alternative Option (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4]**

#### (a) Describe the metabolic engineering strategies for altering nitrogen metabolism, with special emphasis on amino acid overproduction. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Introduction (1 mark):** Nitrogen metabolism centers on the GS-GOGAT cycle, which channels NH₄⁺ into amino acids and nucleotides. Amino acids represent one of the largest tonnage fermentation products globally (>3 million tonnes/yr for L-lysine alone).

2. **C. glutamicum Glutamate Overproduction (2 marks):** Wild-type requires **biotin limitation** to leak glutamate through the membrane (a passive efflux phenomenon). Engineered strains now use **efflux transporter NA** or **odhI attenuation** (disabling ODHC) to constitutively secrete glutamate.

3. **Lysine Pathway Engineering (2 marks):**
   - Feedback-resistant *lysC*^T311I^.
   - *dapA* amplification.
   - *lysE* exporter overexpression.
   - *thrB/C* attenuation (reduce threonine drain).

4. **Threonine Production (1 mark):** *E. coli* with Δ*metL*, Δ*lysC*, attenuated *thrA* produces > 80 g/L L-threonine.

5. **Conclusion (1 mark):** Alteration of nitrogen metabolism is achieved through a combination of enzyme-level deregulation, transporter engineering, and global regulatory rewiring.

#### (b) Explain bioconversions in metabolic engineering with at least two industrial examples. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Definition (1 mark):** Bioconversion (biotransformation) is the use of whole microbial cells or their enzymes to catalyze a specific chemical modification of an externally supplied substrate. Unlike fermentation, the substrate is a complex, preformed molecule (e.g., steroid, sorbitol).

2. **Example 1 — L-Sorbose Production (2 marks):** *Gluconobacter oxydans* oxidizes D-sorbitol → L-sorbose using a membrane-bound PQQ-dependent dehydrogenase. This is the **first step** of the Reichstein process for vitamin C synthesis. Industrial scale reaches 200+ g/L titer.

3. **Example 2 — Steroid Bioconversion (2 marks):** *Rhizopus arrhizus* and *Aspergillus niger* hydroxylate progesterone at the 11α-position, producing 11α-hydroxyprogesterone — a key intermediate for oral contraceptive synthesis. The cytochrome P450 monooxygenase requires O₂ and NADH regeneration, typically via glucose co-substrate.

4. **Example 3 — L-Phenylacetylcarbinol (1 mark):** *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* converts benzaldehyde + pyruvate → L-PAC (precursor of ephedrine), via pyruvate decarboxylase (PDC).

5. **Industrial Significance (1 mark):** Bioconversions enable stereospecific, regio-selective, and enantioselective chemistry that is often impossible or environmentally wasteful via traditional organic synthesis — making them pillars of green chemistry and pharmaceutical manufacturing.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Typically Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing fermentation with bioconversion.** Fermentation = *de novo* synthesis from simple carbon; bioconversion = modification of an externally supplied substrate. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 2. **Skipping yield equations.** Any 7-mark question asking about "product yield enhancement" *must* include $Y_{P/S}$, $q_P$, and at least one stoichiometric calculation. Marks are deducted when only qualitative strategies are listed.
> 3. **Omitting case studies.** Naming strategies without anchoring them to **specific case studies** (e.g., "lysine overproduction via feedback-resistant lysC in C. glutamicum") loses marks. KTU examiners reward named strains and genes.
> 4. **Mixing up modules.** DEBS is a Type I PKS — students commonly mislabel it as Type II. *Type II* (iterative, e.g., actinorhodin) is for aromatic polyketides.
> 5. **Forgetting cofactor balance.** Lysine needs 4 NADPH per mole; riboflavin needs FAD. Examiners expect explicit mention of cofactor engineering.
> 6. **Over-emphasizing one application.** A 14-mark question typically expects coverage across ≥ 2 application areas; students who dwell only on antibiotics miss the breadth expectation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Print-Friendly):**

- **Product Yield:** $Y_{P/S}$, $q_P$, productivity, titer, C-mol yield. Industrial strains target 60–80% of theoretical maximum.
- **5-Tier Engineering Cascade:** Precursor → Bottleneck → Competing branch → Feedback → Transporter.
- **GS-GOGAT Cycle:** Central NH₄⁺ assimilation. GS (ATP-dependent) → Gln; GOGAT (NADPH) → 2 Glu.
- **Lysine Engineering:** *lysC*^T311I^ (feedback-resistant) + *lysE* (exporter) + *dapA* amplification in *C. glutamicum*.
- **Glutamate Production:** Biotin-limited *C. glutamicum* — passive membrane leakage + odhI attenuation.
- **Penicillin Pathway:** PAA (phenylacetyl-CoA ligase) → IPN (IPNS) → Pen G (IAT acyltransferase).
- **Erythromycin:** Type I PKS (DEBS), 6 modules, propionate starter + 6× methylmalonyl-CoA → 6-dEB → erythromycin A.
- **Riboflavin:** GTP cyclohydrolase II rate-limiting; *ribC* (repressor) knockout; *A. gossypii* / *B. subtilis* as hosts.
- **Vitamin B₁₂:** ~30 enzymatic steps; *Pseudomonas denitrificans*; corrin ring + DMB + aminopropanol assembly.
- **Bioconversion Hallmarks:** Single-step or few-step modification; whole-cell or enzyme catalysis; substrate is complex; product is structurally related to substrate.
- **L-Sorbose:** *Gluconobacter oxydans*, PQQ-dependent, first step of Reichstein vitamin C process.
- **Steroid 11α-Hydroxylation:** *Rhizopus arrhizus*, P450 monooxygenase, key for oral contraceptive manufacture.
- **Global Regulators for Engineering:** *crp*, *relA*, *spoT*, *pho*, *fnr* — primary targets for *in silico* prediction of mutation.
- **Cofactor Balance is Critical:** Always check NADH/NADPH demand for a target pathway.
- **Heterologous Expression Platforms:** *E. coli* (fast), *S. cerevisiae* (eukaryotic), *C. glutamicum* (amino acids), *Streptomyces* (antibiotics).
- **Case Studies to Memorize:** Lysine (*C. glutamicum*, Ohnishi 2002), 6-dEB (*E. coli*, Keating & Khosla 1999), 1,3-PD (*E. coli*, DuPont 2003), Riboflavin (*B. subtilis*, Revuelta 2017), Sorbose (*Gluconobacter*).
